elements.text: Interested Parties
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Interested parties to a transaction include, but are not limited to:
elements.label: text

elements.text: the property seller,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the builder or developer,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the real estate agent or broker,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: any affiliate of the above, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: any party that can benefit from the sale of the property at the highest price and influence the sales price or real estate transaction
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: (For Fannie Mae's purposes, an affiliation exists when there is direct common ownership or control by the lender over the interested party, by the interested party over the lender or by a third party over both.)
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : A lender or employer is not considered an interested party to a sales transaction unless it is the property seller or is affiliated with the property seller or another interested party to the transaction.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Financing Concessions
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Financing concessions are financial contributions towards the loan transaction from interested parties. Financing concessions are acceptable when they are contributed towards:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Borrower closing costs, including prepaids; or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Borrower homeowners' association (HOA) assessments covering any period after the settlement date (limited to no more than 12 months).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Maximum Financing Concessions
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The table below provides maximum financing concessions, which are calculated using the lower of the sales price or appraised value (not the loan amount) of the subject transaction. Typical fees and/or closing costs paid by a seller in accordance with local custom, known as common and customary fees or costs, are not subject to Fannie Mae maximum financing concessions.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Financing concessions that exceed the below limits are considered sales concessions and must be deducted from the property's sales price. As a result, the maximum LTV/CLTV ratios must be recalculated using the reduced sales price or appraised value. Additionally, financing concessions must be equal to or less than the sum of the borrower's closing costs. Any amount exceeding the borrower's closing costs must be treated as a sales concession. See Sales Concessions below.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: See  B5-4.2-03, Loans Secured by HomePath Properties  for an exception to this limit for principal residence transactions. 1

elements.text: Sales Concessions
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Sales concessions are IPCs that take the form of non-realty items and may be paid prior to, at or after closing of the transaction. They include, but are not limited to:
elements.label: text

elements.text: contributions such as cash/cash-like gifts;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: rebates, such as those from real estate agents or brokers, which are not credited towards the transaction;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: furniture, automobiles, decorator allowances, moving costs, and other giveaways;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: lender incentives as described in B3-4.1-03, Lender Incentives from a lender who is, or is affiliated with, an interested party; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: financing concessions that exceed the maximum financing concessions.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Sales concessions must be deducted from the property's sales price and the lower of the reduced sales price or appraised value must be used to calculate LTV/CLTV ratios for underwriting and eligibility purposes.
elements.label: text

elements.text: IPC Exclusions
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The following are not considered to be IPCs and are not subject to the requirements described in this topic.
elements.label: text

elements.text: A lender credit derived from premium pricing, even if the lender is an interested party to the transaction;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Gift funds or gift of equity from a seller who is also an acceptable donor provided that:
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The donor is not a builder, or another interested party, and has no affiliation with any other interested party to the transaction, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: All requirements pertaining to gift funds and gift of equity from an acceptable donor as stated in B3-4.3-04, Personal Gifts  and B3-4.3-05, Gifts of Equity are met;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: A legitimate pro-rated real estate tax credit in places where real estate taxes are paid in arrears; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Fees for standby commitments (refer to Interest Rate Buydowns section below).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Undisclosed IPCs
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Mortgages with undisclosed IPCs are not eligible for sale to Fannie Mae. Examples of these types of contributions include, but are not limited to
elements.label: text

elements.text: moving expenses,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: payment of various fees on the borrower's behalf,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: "silent" second mortgages held by the property seller, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: other contributions that are given to the borrower outside of closing and are not disclosed on the settlement statement.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Interest Rate Buydowns
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: If a temporary or permanent interest rate buydown is being offered to the borrower, and the subsidy is funded by an interested party or a lender affiliated with one, the cost of that subsidy must be included in the IPC calculation.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender must ensure the subsidy cost meets Fannie Mae's allowable maximum financing concessions. This can be accomplished by confirming the current market interest rate (that is, the rate without the payment of any discount points) and the discount points being charged to obtain the interest rate offered with the buydown.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : Standby commitment (also known as forward commitment) agreements between a builder and lender for blanket interest rate coverage that are executed prior to signing a sales contract with a borrower are not subject to Fannie Mae's maximum financing concessions because they are not attributable to the specific loan transaction. Loans with a reduced interest rate due to a standby commitment must be delivered with SFC 887.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Payment Abatements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Loans with any type of payment abatement are not eligible for sale to Fannie Mae, even if they are disclosed on the settlement statement. This prohibition applies when an interested party funds the abatement directly and/or through another entity, such as a nonprofit down payment assistance program.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : The payment of HOA fees is not considered an abatement unless the payment of the fee extends for more than 12 months. Paying HOA fees for 12 months or less is considered an interested party contribution.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lender Checklist for IPCs
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender must ensure that all of the following requirements for an IPC are satisfied.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Lender Checklist for IPCs
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Ensure that any and all IPCs have been identified and taken into consideration.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Provide the appraiser with all appropriate financing data and IPCs for the subject property granted by anyone associated with the transaction.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Ensure that the property value is adequately supported.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Ensure that the LTV and CLTV ratios, after any IPCs are taken into consideration, remain within Fannie Mae's eligibility limits for the particular product.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Ensure that the level of mortgage insurance coverage, if applicable, has been obtained, based on the standard LTV ratio after any IPC adjustments have been made.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : When the transaction is a purchase of a property located in the state of New York, the determination of whether to place mortgage insurance must be based solely on New York state law, using the appraised value at the time of sale for a non-co-op property or the sales price for a co-op property without an adjustment reflecting excess IPC contributions. See LTV Ratio Determination in New York State in B7-1-01, Provision of Mortgage Insurance, for additional information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Scrutinize all loan and sales contract documents, including but not limited to the sales contract, the loan estimate, the loan application, the appraisal report, and the settlement statement.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Ensure that all elements of the settlement statement were taken into consideration during the underwriting process.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Ensure that fees and expenses are consistent between all documents. Analyze any differences and review any discrepancies.
elements.label: text

elements.text: B3-4.1-03, Lender Incentives (07/02/2025)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on lender incentives.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lender Incentives
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Lender Incentives
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender may provide the borrower, either directly or through a third-party, with an incentive such as cash, a cash-like gift (for example, a gift card), or other item of value that is not a lender credit toward the loan transaction. The incentive may be offered on all loans as part of a promotion or lender program, provided that
elements.label: text

elements.text: the incentive is not sourced from the loan transaction (for example, premium pricing),
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the borrower qualifies without consideration of the incentive (for example, the incentive cannot be considered borrower assets, used to reduce the payment on an outstanding credit card account, or included in maximum cash back to borrower at closing calculation), and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: no repayment is required.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: When the lender is, or is affiliated with, an interested party to the transaction, the incentive must be treated as a sales concession.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : If the lender is providing a credit toward the borrower's closing costs and/or prepaid items, those funds are considered a lender contribution (not a lender incentive). See B3-4.3-06, Grants and Lender Contributions, for information about lender contributions.
elements.label: text

elements.text: B3-4.1-04, Virtual Currency (05/04/2022)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on the use of virtual currency, such as cryptocurrencies, as part of the asset assessment process.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Virtual Currency
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Virtual Currency
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Virtual currency that has been exchanged into U.S. dollars is acceptable for the down payment, closing costs, and financial reserves provided the following requirements are met:
elements.label: text

elements.text: there is documented evidence that the virtual currency has been exchanged into U.S. dollars and is held in a U.S. or state regulated financial institution, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the funds are verified in U.S. dollars prior to the loan closing.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: A large deposit may be from virtual currency that was exchanged into U.S. dollars. The lender must obtain sufficient documentation to verify the funds originated from the borrower's virtual currency account.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Virtual currency may not be used for the deposit on the sales contract (earnest money) for the purchase of the subject property.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Section B3-4.2, Verification of Depository Assets
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: B3-4.2-01, Verification of Deposits and Assets (05/04/2022)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on verifying deposits and assets, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Verification of Deposits and Assets
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Asset Documentation Provided by a Third-Party Asset Verification Vendor
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Blanket Authorization Form
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Verification of Deposits and Assets
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender can use any of the following types of documentation to verify that a borrower has sufficient funds for closing, down payment, and/or financial reserves:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Request for Verification of Deposit (Form 1006). The information must be requested directly from the depository institution, and the complete, signed, and dated document must be sent directly from the depository institution.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Copies of bank statements or investment portfolio statements. All statements must:
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: clearly identify the financial institution,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: clearly identify the borrower as the account holder,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: include at least the last four digits of the account number,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: include the time period covered by the statement,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: include all deposits and withdrawal transactions (for depository accounts),
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: include all purchase and sale transactions (for financial portfolio accounts), and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: include the ending account balance.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: If the lender is the holder of the borrower's account, the lender may produce a printout or other alternative verification of the asset(s) directly from its system. The printout or alternative verification is acceptable as long as all required data (above) is supplied and documented.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Copies of retirement account statements. They must be the most recent statements, and they must identify the borrower's vested amount and the terms. (See B3-4.3-03, Retirement Accounts, for additional information.)
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The number of required bank or investment portfolio statements varies per transaction type as shown in the following table.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Documentation Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Purchase transactions
elements.label: text

elements.text: The statements must cover the most recent full two-month period of account activity (60 days, or, if account information is reported on a quarterly basis, the most recent quarter).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Refinance transactions
elements.label: text

elements.text: The statements must cover the most recent full one-month period of account activity (30 days, or, if account information is reported on a quarterly basis, the most recent quarter).
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the latest bank statement is more than 45 days earlier than the date of the loan application, the lender should ask the borrower to provide a more recent, supplemental, bank-generated form that shows at least the last four digits of the account number, balance, and date. The statements may be computer-generated forms, including online account or portfolio statements downloaded by the borrower from the Internet.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Documents that are faxed to the lender or downloaded from the Internet must clearly identify the name of the depository or investment institution and the source of information-for example, by including that information in the Internet or fax banner at the top of the document.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If necessary, the lender must supplement these verifications by obtaining any missing information from the borrower or the depository institution.
elements.label: text

elements.text: See  B3-4.4-02, Requirements for Certain Assets in DU, for additional information about documentation of assets for DU loan casefiles.
elements.label: text

elements.text: In addition, loans with assets validated by DU must comply with all requirements pertaining to the DU validation service. Compliance with the DU messages satisfies the requirement for documenting assets. This documentation may differ from the requirements described above. See  B3-2-02, DU Validation Service , for additional information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Asset Documentation Provided by a Third-Party Asset Verification Vendor
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Direct verification by a third-party asset verification vendor. These verifications are acceptable as long as:
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the borrower provided proper authorizations for the lender to use the verification method,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the verified information provided must conform with the information that would be provided on
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Form 1006 or on bank statements,
elements.label: text

elements.text: the date of the completed verification is in compliance with B1-1-03, Allowable Age of Credit Documents and Federal Income Tax Returns,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the lender has determined that the vendor maintains reasonable practices that ensure reliable and authorized verifications of deposit and asset information (see A4-1-01, Maintaining Seller/Servicer Eligibility), and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the lender understands it will be held accountable for the integrity of the information obtained from this source.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Blanket Authorization Form
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Rather than having the applicant sign multiple forms, the lender may have the applicant sign an authorization form which gives the lender blanket authorization to request the information it needs to evaluate the applicant's creditworthiness. (See  B1-1-02, Blanket Authorization Form.) When the lender uses this type of blanket authorization, it must attach a copy of the authorization form to each Form 1006 it sends to the depository institutions in which the applicant has accounts.
elements.label: text

elements.text: B3-4.2-02, Depository Accounts (12/14/2022)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on depository accounts, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Depository Accounts
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Business Assets
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Evaluating Large Deposits
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Request for Verification of Deposit
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Depository Accounts
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Funds held in a checking, savings, money market, certificate of deposit, or other depository accounts may be used for the down payment, closing costs, and financial reserves. The funds must be verified as described in B3-4.2-01, Verification of Deposits and Assets. Unverified funds are not acceptable for the down payment, closing costs, or financial reserves.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender must investigate any indications of borrowed funds. These must be identified differently based upon how the asset account was verified.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Business Assets
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Business assets may be an acceptable source of funds for the down payment, closing costs, and financial reserves. The borrower must be listed as an owner of the account and the account must be verified in accordance with  B3-4.2-01, Verification of Deposits and Assets. If the borrower is also using self-employment income from this business to qualify, see Section B3-3.5, Self-Employment Income, for additional information on the analysis of a self-employed borrower.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Evaluating Large Deposits
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A large deposit is defined as a single deposit that exceeds 50% of the total monthly qualifying income for the loan.  When bank statements (typically covering the most recent two months) are used, the lender must evaluate large deposits. See B3-4.1-04, Virtual Currency for additional information when a large deposit may be from virtual currency that was exchanged into U.S. dollars. Requirements for evaluating large deposits vary based on the transaction type, as shown in the table below.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Transaction Type
elements.label: text

elements.text: Evaluation Requirements
elements.label: text

elements.text: Refinance transactions
elements.label: text

elements.text: Documentation or explanation for large deposits is
elements.label: text

elements.text: not
elements.label: text

elements.text: required; however,
elements.label: text

elements.text: the lender remains responsible for ensuring that any borrowed funds,
elements.label: text

elements.text: including any related liability, are considered.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Transaction Type
elements.label: text

elements.text: Evaluation Requirements
elements.label: text

elements.text: Purchase transactions
elements.label: text

elements.text: • If funds from a large deposit are needed to complete the purchase
elements.label: text

elements.text: transaction (that is, are used for the down payment, closing costs, or
elements.label: text

elements.text: financial reserves), the lender must document that those funds are from an
elements.label: text

elements.text: acceptable source. Occasionally, a borrower may not have all of the
elements.label: text

elements.text: documentation required to confirm the source of a deposit. In those
elements.label: text

elements.text: instances, the lender must use reasonable judgment based on the available
elements.label: text

elements.text: documentation as well as the borrower's debt-to-income ratio and overall
elements.label: text

elements.text: income and credit profile. Examples of acceptable documentation include
elements.label: text

elements.text: the borrower's written explanation, proof of ownership of an asset that was
elements.label: text

elements.text: sold, or a copy of a wedding invitation to support receipt of gift funds. The
elements.label: text

elements.text: lender must place in the loan file written documentation of the rationale for
elements.label: text

elements.text: using the funds.
elements.label: text

elements.text: • Verified funds must be reduced by the amount (or portion) of the
elements.label: text

elements.text: undocumented large deposit (as defined above), and the lender must
elements.label: text

elements.text: confirm that the remaining funds are sufficient for the down payment,
elements.label: text

elements.text: closing costs, and financial reserves. When the lender uses a reduced asset
elements.label: text

elements.text: amount, net of the unsourced amount of a large deposit, that reduced
elements.label: text

elements.text: amount must be used for underwriting purposes (whether the loan is
elements.label: text

elements.text: underwritten manually or through DU).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note
elements.label: text

elements.text: : When a deposit has both sourced and unsourced portions, only the
elements.label: text

elements.text: unsourced portion must be used to calculate whether or not it must be
elements.label: text

elements.text: considered a large deposit.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Examples
elements.label: text

elements.text: Scenario 1:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Borrower has monthly income of $4,000 and an account at
elements.label: text

elements.text: ABC Bank with a balance of $20,000. A deposit of $3,000 is identified, but
elements.label: text

elements.text: $2,500 of that deposit is documented as coming from the borrower's federal
elements.label: text

elements.text: income tax refund.Only the unsourced $500 [the deposit of $3,000 minus
elements.label: text

elements.text: the documented $2,500] must be considered in calculating whether it meets
elements.label: text

elements.text: the large deposit definition.The unsourced $500 is 12.5% of the borrower's
elements.label: text

elements.text: $4,000 monthly income, falling short of the 50% definition of a large
elements.label: text

elements.text: deposit.Therefore, it is not considered a large deposit and the entire
elements.label: text

elements.text: $20,000 balance in the ABC Bank account can be used for underwriting
elements.label: text

elements.text: purposes.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Scenario 2:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Using the same borrower example, a deposit of $3,000 is
elements.label: text

elements.text: identified, but only $500 is documented as coming from the borrower's
elements.label: text

elements.text: federal income tax refund, leaving $2,500 unsourced.In this instance, the
elements.label: text

elements.text: unsourced $2,500 is 63% of the borrower's $4,000 monthly income, which
elements.label: text

elements.text: does meet the definition of a large deposit.Therefore, the unsourced $2,500
elements.label: text

elements.text: must be subtracted from the account balance of $20,000 and only the
elements.label: text

elements.text: remaining $17,500 may be used for underwriting purposes.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Note : If the source of a large deposit is readily identifiable on the account statement(s), such as a direct deposit from an employer (payroll), the Social Security Administration, or IRS or state income tax refund, or a transfer of funds between verified accounts, and the source of the deposit is printed on the statement, the lender does not need to obtain further explanation or documentation. However, if the source of the deposit is printed on the statement, but the lender still has questions as to whether the funds may have been borrowed, the lender should obtain additional documentation.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The DU validation service automates the assessment of large deposits. When assets are validated, DU issues a message indicating which large deposits require documentation. Compliance with the DU messages satisfies the requirement for documenting large deposits. See B3-2-02, DU Validation Service
elements.label: text

elements.text: Request for Verification of Deposit
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When a Verification of Deposit (Form 1006) (VOD) is used and depository activity is not included, the lender must verify the source of funds for
elements.label: text

elements.text: accounts opened within the last 90 days of the application date, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: account balances that are considerably greater than the average balance reflected on the VOD.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Recent Related Announcements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The table below provides references to recently issued Announcements that are related to this topic.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Announcements
elements.label: text

elements.text: Issue Date
elements.label: text

elements.text: Announcement SEL-2022-10
elements.label: text

elements.text: B3-4.2-03, Individual Development Accounts (02/06/2019)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on individual development accounts, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Individual Development Accounts
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Use of IDA Funds to Meet Borrower Minimum Contribution Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Lender Checklist for IDAs
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Individual Development Accounts
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Some nonprofit agencies will match the funds a borrower regularly deposits into a savings account that has been designated as an account that is used solely for the accumulation of funds to purchase a home. Such accounts are referred to as individual development accounts, or IDAs.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Nonprofit agencies that offer IDA programs have options with respect to accumulating and holding the matching funds, which include:
elements.label: text

elements.text: the use of a parallel 'savings' account that is separate from the homebuyer's savings account;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: separately designated matching funds within a single agency account via accounting processes to allocate matching funds to a particular homebuyer; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the use of a trustee account that contains both the homebuyer's funds and the agency's matching funds.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: When a homebuyer reaches the target amount and is ready to complete the home purchase, the funds are disbursed from the nonprofit agency account to the closing agent via a single check or multiple checks.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the agency's matching funds are held in an account that is separate from the homebuyer's account, the matching funds need not be commingled with the homebuyer's funds prior to disbursement to the closing agent. It is acceptable to allow the separate disbursement of funds from the agency and from the homebuyer, as long as the terms of the IDA program are met.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Funds that the borrower deposited into an IDA may be used for either closing costs or the down payment.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Use of IDA Funds to Meet Borrower Minimum Contribution Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Funds that the borrower deposited into an IDA may be used for either the closing costs or the down payment. Depending on the repayment terms of the IDA program, the borrower may or may not be required to meet the minimum down payment requirements from their own funds, as outlined below:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: IDA Repayment Terms
elements.label: text

elements.text: Allowable Use of Matching Funds
elements.label: text

elements.text: The nonprofit agency
elements.label: text

elements.text: • requires repayment of the matching funds,
elements.label: text

elements.text: • agrees to defer or forgive repayment provided
elements.label: text

elements.text: that certain conditions are met, or
elements.label: text

elements.text: • files a lien against the property.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The borrower may use the matching funds to
elements.label: text

elements.text: supplement the down payment provided they have
elements.label: text

elements.text: met the minimum borrower contribution
elements.label: text

elements.text: requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The minimum borrower contribution must come from
elements.label: text

elements.text: the borrower's own funds unless:
elements.label: text

elements.text: • the LTV or CLTV ratio is less than or equal to 80%;
elements.label: text

elements.text: or
elements.label: text

elements.text: • the borrower is purchasing a one-unit principal
elements.label: text

elements.text: residence and meets the requirements to use gifts,
elements.label: text

elements.text: donated grant funds, or funds received from an
elements.label: text

elements.text: employer to pay for some or all of the borrower's
elements.label: text

elements.text: minimum contributions. See B3-4.3-04, Personal Gifts;
elements.label: text

elements.text: B3-4.3-06, Grants and Lender Contributions; and
elements.label: text

elements.text: B3-4.3-08, Employer Assistance, for additional
elements.label: text

elements.text: information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The nonprofit agency
elements.label: text

elements.text: • does not require repayment of the matching
elements.label: text

elements.text: funds and
elements.label: text

elements.text: • does not file a lien against the property.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The borrower may use the matching funds for some
elements.label: text

elements.text: or all of the down payment without first being
elements.label: text

elements.text: required to meet the minimum borrower contribution
elements.label: text

elements.text: requirement from their own funds.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lender Checklist for IDAs
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender must ensure that all of the following requirements for an IDA are satisfied:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lender Checklist for IDAs
elements.label: text

elements.text: Document how the nonprofit agency's IDA program operates.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Verify the rate at which the agency matches borrower deposits into the account.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Determine that the borrower satisfied the program's vesting requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Document the borrower's regular payments into the account and the agency's regular
elements.label: text

elements.text: deposits of matching funds into the account.
elements.label: text

elements.text: B3-4.2-04, Pooled Savings (Community Savings Funds) (04/01/2009)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on pooled savings (community savings funds).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Pooled Savings (Community Savings Funds)
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Pooled Savings (Community Savings Funds)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Funds from a community savings account or any other type of pooled savings may be used for the down payment if the borrower can document regular contributions to the fund.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Acceptable documentation includes written confirmation from the party managing the pooled savings fund and documentation of regular borrower contributions.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The borrower's obligation to continue making contributions to the fund must be considered as part of the borrower's debt when calculating the total debt-to-income ratio.
elements.label: text

elements.label: text

elements.text: B3-4.2-05, Foreign Assets (05/04/2022)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic provides information on the use of assets that are located outside the United States and its territories.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Requirements for Foreign Assets
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Requirements for Foreign Assets
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender must document all sources of funds used for down payments, closing costs and financial reserves. All documents of a foreign origin must be completed in English, or the originator must provide a translation, attached to each document, and ensure the translation is complete and accurate.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When the source of those funds originates from assets located outside of the U.S. and its territories, those assets require
elements.label: text

elements.text: documented evidence of the foreign assets exchanged into U.S. dollars and held in a U.S. or state regulated financial institution, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: verification of the funds in U.S. dollars prior to the loan closing.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The lender must evaluate large deposits in accordance with  B3-4.2-02, Depository Accounts.
elements.label: text

elements.text: B3-4.3-01, Stocks, Stock Options, Bonds, and Mutual Funds (06/30/2015)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on stocks, stock options, bonds, and mutual funds.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Stocks, Stock Options, Bonds, and Mutual Funds
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Stocks, Stock Options, Bonds, and Mutual Funds
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Vested assets in the form of stocks, government bonds, and mutual funds are acceptable sources of funds for the down payment, closing costs, and reserves provided their value can be verified. The lender must verify the borrower's ownership of the account or asset. The value of the asset and any related documentation must meet the requirements outlined in the table below.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Asset Type
elements.label: text

elements.text: Determining the Value of the Asset
elements.label: text

elements.text: Stocks and mutual funds
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender must determine the value of the asset (net of any margin
elements.label: text

elements.text: accounts) by obtaining either
elements.label: text

elements.text: • the most recent monthly or quarterly statement from the depository or
elements.label: text

elements.text: investment firm; or
elements.label: text

elements.text: • a copy of the stock certificate, accompanied by a newspaper stock list
elements.label: text

elements.text: that is dated as of or near the date of the loan application.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Stock options
elements.label: text

elements.text: The value of vested stock options can be documented by
elements.label: text

elements.text: • a statement that lists the number of options and the option price, and
elements.label: text

elements.text: • using the current stock price to determine the gain that would be realized
elements.label: text

elements.text: from exercise of an option and the sale of the optioned stock.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note
elements.label: text

elements.text: : Non-vested stock options are not an acceptable source of funds for
elements.label: text

elements.text: the down payment, closing costs, or reserves and should not be entered on
elements.label: text

elements.text: the loan application.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Government bonds
elements.label: text

elements.text: The value of government bonds must be based on their purchase price
elements.label: text

elements.text: unless the redemption value can be documented.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When used for the down payment or closing costs, if the value of the asset (as determined above) is at least 20% more than the amount of funds needed for the down payment and closing costs, no documentation of the borrower's actual receipt of funds realized from the sale or liquidation is required. Otherwise, evidence of the borrower's actual receipt of funds realized from the sale or liquidation must be documented.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When used for reserves, 100% of the value of the assets (as determined above) may be considered, and liquidation is not required.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Refer to B3-4.3-03, Retirement Accounts, for the requirements pertaining to the use of retirement accounts for the down payment, closing costs, or reserves.
elements.label: text

elements.text: B3-4.3-02, Trust Accounts (04/01/2009)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: This topic contains information on trust accounts.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Trust Accounts
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Trust Accounts
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Funds disbursed from a borrower's trust account are an acceptable source for the down payment, closing costs, and reserves provided the borrower has immediate access to the funds.
elements.label: text

elements.text: To document trust account funds, the lender must:
elements.label: text

elements.text: obtain written documentation of the value of the trust account from either the trust manager or the trustee, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: document the conditions under which the borrower has access to the funds and the effect, if any, that the withdrawal of funds will have on trust income used in qualifying the borrower for the mortgage.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: B3-4.3-03, Retirement Accounts (06/30/2015)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on retirement accounts.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Retirement Accounts
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Retirement Accounts
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Vested funds from individual retirement accounts (IRA/SEP/Keogh accounts) and tax-favored retirement savings accounts (401(k) accounts) are acceptable sources of funds for the down payment, closing costs, and reserves. The lender must verify the ownership of the account and confirm that the account is vested and allows withdrawals regardless of current employment status.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the retirement assets are in the form of stocks, bonds, or mutual funds, the account must meet the requirements of B3-4.3-01, Stocks, Stock Options, Bonds, and Mutual Funds, for determining value and whether documentation of the borrower's actual receipt of funds is required when used for the down payment and closing costs. When funds from retirement accounts are used for reserves, Fannie Mae does not require the funds to be withdrawn from the account(s).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Recent Related Announcements There are no recently issued Announcements related to this topic.
elements.label: text

elements.text: B3-4.3-04, Personal Gifts (02/04/2026)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on personal gifts, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Gift Funds
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Acceptable Donors
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Minimum Borrower Contribution Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Documentation Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Verifying Donor Availability of Funds and Transfer of Gift Funds
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Gift Funds
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A borrower of a mortgage loan secured by a principal residence or second home may use funds received as a personal gift from an acceptable donor. Gift funds may fund all or part of the down payment, closing costs, or financial reserves subject to the minimum borrower contribution requirements below. Gifts are not allowed on an investment property.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : A gift of equity may not be used for financial reserves. For additional information, see B3-4.3-05, Gifts of Equity.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Acceptable Donors
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A gift can be provided by:
elements.label: text

elements.text: a relative, defined as the borrower's spouse, child, or other dependent, or by any other individual who is related to the borrower by blood, marriage, adoption, or legal guardianship; or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a non-relative that shares a familial relationship with the borrower defined as a domestic partner (or relative of the domestic partner), individual engaged to marry the borrower, former relative, or an individual with a long-standing familial-like or mentorship relationship with the borrower.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The donor may not be, or have any affiliation with, the builder, the developer, the real estate agent, or any other interested party to the transaction.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : Gift funds from the seller who is also an acceptable donor and not affiliated with any other interested party to the transaction are allowed. The donor of a gift of equity is not considered an interested party to the transaction.
elements.label: text

elements.text: See  B3-4.3-06, Grants and Lender Contributions, for information about donations from entities (grants).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Documentation Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Gifts must be evidenced by a letter signed by the donor, called a gift letter. When the gift is sourced by a trust established by an acceptable donor or an estate of an acceptable donor, the gift letter must be signed by the donor and list the name of the trust or the estate account.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The gift letter must:
elements.label: text

elements.text: specify the actual or the maximum dollar amount of the gift;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: include the donor's statement that no repayment is expected; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: indicate the donor's name, address, telephone number, and relationship to the borrower.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Note: If the actual amount of the gift received is different than the amount used to underwrite the loan casefile in DU, the lender may need to resubmit the loan casefile to DU in accordance with B3-2-10, Accuracy of DU Data, DU Tolerances, and Errors in the Credit Report. For manually underwritten loans, the lender must verify the borrower has sufficient funds for closing, down payment and/or financial reserves.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When a gift from an acceptable donor is being pooled with the borrower's funds to make up the required minimum cash down payment, the following items must also be included:
elements.label: text

elements.text: A certification from the donor stating that they have lived with the borrower for the past 12 months and will continue to do so in the new residence.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Documents that demonstrate a history of borrower and donor shared residency. The donor's address must be the same as the borrower's address. Examples include but are not limited to a copy of a driver's license, a bill, or a bank statement.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Verifying Donor Availability of Funds and Transfer of Gift Funds
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender must verify that sufficient funds to cover the gift are either in the donor's account (such as a checking, savings or investment account, or trust or estate account owned by the donor) or have been transferred to the borrower's account. Acceptable documentation includes the following:
elements.label: text

elements.text: a copy of the donor's check and the borrower's deposit slip,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a copy of the donor's withdrawal slip and the borrower's deposit slip,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: evidence of the electronic transfer of funds from the donor's account to the borrower's account or to the closing agent;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a copy of the donor's check to the closing agent, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a settlement statement showing receipt of the donor's check.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: When the funds are not transferred prior to settlement, the lender must document that the donor gave the closing agent the gift funds in the form of an electronic transfer, certified check, a cashier's check, or other official check.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the borrower receives a gift from an acceptable donor who has lived with the borrower for the last 12 months, the gift is considered the borrower's own funds and may be used to satisfy the minimum borrower contribution requirement as long as both individuals will use the home being purchased as their principal residence. 1

elements.text: B3-4.3-05, Gifts of Equity (10/07/2020)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on gifts of equity, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Gift of Equity
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Documentation Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Gift of Equity
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A 'gift of equity' refers to a gift provided by the seller of a property to the buyer. The gift represents a portion of the seller's equity in the property, and is transferred to the buyer as a credit in the transaction.
elements.label: text

elements.text: A gift of equity
elements.label: text

elements.text: is permitted for principal residence and second home purchase transactions;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: can be used to fund all or part of the down payment and closing costs (including prepaid items); and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: cannot be used towards financial reserves.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The acceptable donor and minimum borrower contribution requirements for gifts also apply to gifts of equity. See B3-4.3-04, Personal Gifts. When a gift of equity is provided by an acceptable donor, the donor is not considered to be an interested party and the gift of equity is not subject to Fannie Mae's interested party contribution requirements (see B3-4.1-02, Interested Party Contributions (IPCs)).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Documentation Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The following documents must be retained in the loan file:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: a signed gift letter (see B3-4.3-04, Personal Gifts), and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the settlement statement listing the gift of equity.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: B3-4.3-06, Grants and Lender Contributions (05/07/2025)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on grants and lender contributions, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Donations From Entities - Grants
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Minimum Borrower Contribution Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Documentation Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Lender-funded Grants
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Lender Contributions
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Donations From Entities - Grants
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Borrowers of a loan secured by a principal residence may use funds donated from acceptable entities for all or part of the down payment, closing costs, or financial reserves subject to the minimum borrower contribution requirements described below. These funds are referred to as a grant.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Grants must be funded by one of the following entities, provided they are not the property seller or other interested party in the transaction:
elements.label: text

elements.text: a federal agency, state, county, or similar political subdivision of a state;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: any city, town, village, or borough of a state that
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: has a local government and that has been created by a special legislative act,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: has been otherwise individually incorporated or chartered pursuant to state law, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: is recognized as such under the constitution or by the laws of the state in which it is located,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a housing finance agency as defined in 24 C.F.R. §266.5;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a nonprofit organization exempt from taxation under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a regional Federal Home Loan Bank under one of its affordable housing programs;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: an employer where the borrower is an employee (see B3-4.3-08, Employer Assistance);
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: an Indian tribe on the most current list published by the Secretary of the Interior pursuant to 25 U.S.C. §5131; or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a lender (see Lender-funded Grants ).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Down payment assistance may not be funded in any way through the first mortgage, such as through premium pricing.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Grant funds may also be applied towards energy-related improvements if
elements.label: text

elements.text: the program under which the funds are made available allows such a use, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Lender Contributions
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender may provide the borrower with a contribution to fund borrower-paid closing costs and prepaid fees in the following cases:
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender credit is derived from premium pricing, as described in Premium Pricing in B2-1.5-02, Loan Eligibility.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The lender credit is sourced directly from lender funds with no expectation for repayment or financial obligation apart from the subject mortgage. Funds passed to the lender from a third party, for the purpose of providing a lender credit, are not eligible as a lender contribution.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The amount of the lender contribution should not exceed the amount of borrower-paid closing costs and prepaid fees and may not be used to fund any portion of the down payment or financial reserve requirements. Lender contributions are not considered grants.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.label: text

elements.text: Any excess lender credit required to be returned to the borrower in accordance with applicable regulatory requirements is considered an overpayment of fees and charges and may be applied as a principal curtailment or returned in cash to the borrower. See  B2-1.3-01, Purchase Transactions and B2-1.3-02, Limited Cash-Out Refinance Transactions for treatment of the overpayment of fees and charges.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lenders may also provide borrowers with cash or cash-like incentives that do not need to be reflected on the settlement statement. See  B3-4.1-02, Interested Party Contributions (IPCs) for additional information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: If a lender is required to provide down payment assistance to satisfy a legal settlement or judgement, enforcement action or other regulatory action, Fannie Mae will consider purchasing those loans on a negotiated basis. Lenders may contact their Fannie Mae customer account team for more information.

elements.label: list_item

elements.label: list_item

elements.text: A borrower of a mortgage loan secured by a principal residence may use funds provided by an employer to fund all or part of the down payment or closing costs subject to the minimum borrower contribution requirements below. Employer assistance can also be used for financial reserves for all types of assistance with the exception of unsecured loans (which may only be used for the down payment and closing costs). Employer assistance funds are not allowed on a second home or an investment property.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Funds must come directly from the employer, including through an employer-affiliated credit union.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When employer assistance is extended as a secured second mortgage, the transaction may be structured as a Community Seconds (see B5-5.1-02, Community Seconds Loan Eligibility) or it must satisfy Fannie Mae's eligibility criteria for mortgages that are subject to subordinate financing (see B2-1.2-04, Subordinate Financing).

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: If the secured second mortgage or unsecured loan does not require regular payments of either principal and interest or interest only, the lender does not need to calculate an equivalent payment for consideration as part of the borrower's monthly debt. If regular payments are required for the secured second mortgage, the payments must be included in the calculation of the debt-to-income ratio.

elements.text: B3-4.3-09, Earnest Money Deposit (05/04/2022)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on the earnest money deposit, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Sales Contract Deposit
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Verification of Source of Funds
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Documentation for Receipt of the Deposit
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Sales Contract Deposit
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The deposit on the sales contract (earnest money) for the purchase of the security property is an acceptable source of funds for both the down payment and the closing costs. See  B3-4.1-04, Virtual Currency for additional information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Verification of Source of Funds
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: If the deposit is being used as part of the borrower's minimum contribution requirement, the lender must verify that the funds are from an acceptable source. See  B3-4.2-01, Verification of Deposits and Assets.
elements.label: text

elements.text: A Request for Verification of Deposit (Form 1006) must indicate that the average balance for the past two months was large enough to support the amount of the deposit.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Bank statements must evidence that the average balance for the past two months was large enough to support the amount of the deposit. If a copy of the canceled deposit check is used to document the source of funds, the bank statements must cover the period up to (and including) the date the check cleared the bank account.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If it cannot be determined that these funds were withdrawn from the borrower's account, additional verification of the source and evidence that the funds have actually changed hands from the borrower to the seller, the real estate agent, the escrow agent, or the settlement attorney should be provided. Large earnest money deposits and deposits that exceed the amount customary for the area should be closely evaluated.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Documentation for Receipt of the Deposit
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Receipt of the deposit must be verified by either a copy of the borrower's canceled check or a written statement from the holder of the deposit.

elements.label: section_header

elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Trade Equity
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Trade equity is an acceptable source of funds to supplement the borrower's minimum borrower contribution provided the following requirements are met:
elements.label: text

elements.text: The seller's equity contribution for the traded property must be a true-value consideration supported by a current appraisal.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The borrower must make the minimum required contribution from their own funds unless:
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the LTV or CLTV ratio is less than or equal to 80%; or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the borrower is purchasing a one-unit principal residence and meets the requirements to use gifts, donated grant funds, or funds received from an employer to pay for some or all of the borrower's minimum contribution. See B3-4.3-04, Personal Gifts; B3-4.3-06, Grants and Lender Contributions; and B3-4.3-08, Employer Assistance, for additional information.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: These requirements apply to all transactions that involve property trades, including those that are evidenced by two separate contracts that have the buyer and the seller on one contract reversing roles on the second contract.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : Trade equity is entered in the loan application as a credit to the transaction, which will reduce the borrower's required funds to close.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Calculating the Equity Contribution
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The equity contribution is determined by subtracting the outstanding mortgage balance of the property being traded, plus any transfer costs, from the lesser of either the property's appraised value or the trade-in value agreed to by both parties.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For trade equity requirements for manufactured housing, see Section B5-2-03, Manufactured Housing Underwriting Requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Documentation Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: For real property, the transfer deed must be recorded.
elements.label: text

elements.text: In addition, lenders must obtain the following:
elements.label: text

elements.text: A search of the land records to verify the ownership of the property and to determine whether there are any existing liens on the property.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Proof of title transfer and satisfaction of any existing mortgage liens for which the borrower was liable.

elements.text: B3-4.3-12, Rent-Related Credits (08/07/2024)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on different types of rent-related credits, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Rent Credit with an Option to Purchase
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Rent-Back Credit
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Rent Credit with an Option to Purchase
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Rent credit with an option to purchase is the portion of rental payments (also referred to as lease payments) paid by the borrower that can be credited towards the down payment or minimum borrower contribution under a documented rental or purchase agreement. Borrowers are not required to make a minimum borrower contribution from their own funds for the rental payments to be credited toward the down payment. The rent credit is not considered an interested party contribution.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The rent credit from the seller for the down payment is determined by calculating the difference between the market rent and the actual rent paid by the borrower. The market rent is determined by the appraiser in the appraisal for the subject property and the credit may be no more than the difference between the market rent and the actual rent paid.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Documentation Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender must obtain the following documentation:
elements.label: text

elements.text: A copy of the rental/lease with an option to purchase agreement that evidences the following:
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: an original term of at least 12 months,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the total number of months of the agreement,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the monthly rental amount, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the amount of the monthly rent credit.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Copies of the borrower's canceled checks, bank statements, money order receipts or other reasonable methods evidencing the rental payments over the term of the agreement. The documentation must clearly indicate the payee and the amount being paid.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The appraisal of the subject property reflecting the market rent amount.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Rent-Back Credit
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A rent-back credit is an amount paid by the property seller to the borrower in exchange for allowing the seller to stay in the home for a specified period of time after closing. While rent-back credit to the borrower paid by the seller is permissible as part of the sale; it cannot be used as an eligible source of funds for closing costs, down payment, or reserves when qualifying the borrower.
elements.label: text

elements.text: A rent-back credit may appear on the Closing Disclosure as a credit to the borrower. In all cases, the lender must underwrite the loan without any consideration of the rent-back credit (e.g., it must not be entered in the DU loan application data) and must document that the borrower has sufficient funds for the transaction from eligible sources.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: For loans secured by the borrower's principal residence, the borrower must continue to meet any occupancy requirements as outlined in the security instrument.
elements.label: text

elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Borrowed Funds Secured by an Asset
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Borrowed funds secured by an asset are an acceptable source of funds for the down payment, closing costs, and reserves, since borrowed funds secured by an asset represent a return of equity.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Assets that may be used to secure funds include automobiles, artwork, collectibles, real estate, or financial assets, such as savings accounts, certificates of deposit, stocks, bonds, and 401(k) accounts.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Secured Loans as Debt
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When qualifying the borrower, the lender must consider monthly payments for secured loans as a debt.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If a secured loan does not require monthly payments, the lender must calculate an equivalent amount and consider that amount as a recurring debt.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When loans are secured by the borrower's financial assets, monthly payments for the loan do not have to be considered as long-term debt.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Reducing the Asset by the Amount Borrowed
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: If the borrower uses the same financial asset as part of their financial reserves, the lender must reduce the value of the asset by the amount of proceeds and related fees for the secured loan.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Documentation Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender must document the following:
elements.label: text

elements.text: the terms of the secured loan,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: evidence that the party providing the secured loan is not a party to the sale, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: evidence that the funds have been transferred to the borrower.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Recent Related Announcements
elements.label: text

elements.text: There are no recently issued Announcements related to this topic.
elements.label: text

elements.text: B3-4.3-16, Credit Card Financing and Reward Points (08/04/2021)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on the following:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Credit Card Financing
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Credit Card Reward Points
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Credit Card Financing
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Fannie Mae permits certain costs that must be paid early in the application process, such as lock-in fees, origination fees, commitment fees, credit report fees, and appraisal fees, to be charged to the borrower's credit card because these fees do not represent extraordinary amounts and the credit card debt is considered in the borrower's total monthly debt-to-income ratio. Borrowers are not required to pay off these credit card changes before closing. Under no circumstances may credit card financing be used for the down payment.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Lenders may allow credit card financing for the payment of common and customary fees paid outside of closing up to a maximum of 2% of the loan amount if the lender:
elements.label: text

elements.text: confirms that the borrower has sufficient liquid funds (financial reserves) to cover these charges (in addition to funds needed for other closing costs and the down payment that they will be paying); or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: recalculates the credit card payment, per B3-6-05, Monthly Debt Obligations, to account for the new charges and includes the updated payment in the qualifying ratio calculation.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: For DU, lenders must apply this policy manually. The fees charged to the borrower's credit card must be included as a closing cost in the loan application, and removed from any Borrower Paid Fees entered as an other credit for the fees paid outside of closing. Alternatively, the monthly credit card payment in the liabilities section of the loan application must be increased to include the charges if not reflected in the credit report.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Credit Card Reward Points
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Fannie Mae permits credit card reward points as acceptable funds for use towards closing costs, down payment and financial reserves, provided the reward points are converted to cash prior to the closing of the loan. The following requirements apply:
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the credit card reward points are converted to cash and deposited into the borrower's depository account (for example, checking or savings), no additional documentation is required unless the deposit is considered a large deposit.  In this event, the lender must follow the requirements in Evaluating Large Deposits in B3-4.2-02, Depository Accounts .
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If the credit card reward points are converted to cash, but not deposited into a borrower's depository account, the lender must provide evidence the reward points were
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: available to the borrower prior to the conversion, including verification of the cash value (for example, credit card reward statement prior to conversion); and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: converted to cash prior to the closing of the loan.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: For DU loan casefiles, if the reward points are not already converted to cash and deposited into a depository account, lenders must enter the cash value of the reward points as an Asset with an Account Type of "Other" and a description of "Liquid Asset". See  B3-4.4-02, Requirements for Certain Assets in DU  for more information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: B3-4.3-18, Sale of Personal Assets (12/16/2020)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on the sale of personal assets, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Sale of Personal Assets
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Documentation Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Sale of Personal Assets
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Proceeds from the sale of personal assets are an acceptable source of funds for the down payment, closing costs, and reserves provided the individual purchasing the asset is not a party to the property sale transaction or the mortgage financing transaction.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Documentation Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender must document the following:
elements.label: text

elements.text: The borrower's ownership of the asset for all asset types that are titled assets, for example automobile title.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The value of the asset, as determined by an independent and reputable source, if the proceeds represent more than 50% of the total monthly income used in qualifying. The lender must use the lesser of the estimated value (as determined by the independent source) or actual sales price when determining the amount of funds for the transaction. For example, a borrower plans to sell their vehicle. The value as determined by an independent source is $10,000; the sales price of the vehicle is $12,000. $10,000 can be added to the borrower's available funds even if the sale has already occured.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The transfer of ownership of the asset, as documented by either a bill of sale or a statement from the purchaser.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The borrower's receipt of the sale proceeds from documents such as deposit slips, bank statements, copies of the purchaser's canceled check or an equivalent payment source.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: B3-4.3-19, Cash Value of Life Insurance (05/27/2014)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on the cash value of life insurance, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Cash Value of Life Insurance and Debt-to-Income Ratios
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Documenting Borrower Receipt of Funds
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Cash Value of Life Insurance and Debt-to-Income Ratios
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Net proceeds from a loan against the cash value or from the surrender of a life insurance policy are an acceptable source of funds for the down payment, closing costs, and reserves.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender must assess repayment or additional obligation considerations to determine the impact on borrower qualification or reserves.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If penalties for failure to repay the loan are limited to the surrender of the policy, payments on a loan secured by the cash value of a borrower's life insurance policy do not have to be considered in the total debt-to-income ratio.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If additional obligations are indicated, the obligation amount must be factored into the total debt-to-income ratio, or subtracted from the borrower's financial reserves.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Documenting Borrower Receipt of Funds
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: If the funds are needed for the down payment or closing costs, lenders must document the borrower's receipt of the funds from the insurance company by obtaining either a copy of the check from the insurer or a copy of the payout statement issued by the insurer. If the cash-value of the life insurance is being used for reserves, the cash-value must be documented but does not need to be liquidated and received by the borrower.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Recent Related Announcements

elements.text: B3-4.3-20, Anticipated Savings and Cash-on-Hand (04/01/2009)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Anticipated Savings
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Cash-on-Hand
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Anticipated Savings
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender may preliminarily qualify a borrower on the basis that anticipated savings will be sufficient to meet the funds needed for closing. The lender must verify that savings are actually accumulated by the borrower before loan closing.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The estimate for a borrower's anticipated savings must be realistically developed. To calculate potential saved funds, the lender should reduce the borrower's expected after-tax income for the expected savings period by existing housing expenses, monthly debt expenses based on data from the credit report, and expected living expenses, such as food, transportation, etc.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Cash-on-Hand
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Cash-on-hand is not an acceptable source of funds for the down payment or closing costs.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For HomeReady mortgages, cash-on-hand may be considered an acceptable source of funds for the down payment and closing cost. See Chapter B5-6, HomeReady Mortgage.
