elements.text: B3-4.3-21, Borrower's Earned Real Estate Commission (04/05/2023)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on the use of borrower's earned real estate commission as eligible funds for the subject transaction, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Earned Real Estate Commission
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Documentation Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Earned Real Estate Commission
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Earned real estate commission refers to the borrower's portion of a real estate commission earned from the sale of the subject property being purchased when the borrower is acting as their own real estate agent. Lenders may use the borrower's earned real estate commission as an eligible source of funds for down payment and closing costs provided the borrower is a licensed real estate agent and will receive a sales commission from the purchase of the subject property.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Documentation Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender must document the following:
elements.label: text

elements.text: the settlement statement must reflect the commission earned by the borrower, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the earned commission amount must be credited towards the mortgage loan.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Note: For DU loan casefiles, earned real estate commission must be entered as a Credit Type of "Other" in Section 2b of the online loan application.

elements.label: section_header

elements.text: B3-4.4-01, DU Asset Verification (12/16/2020)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on asset verification for loans underwritten with DU, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Liquid Assets
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Non-Liquid Assets
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Reserve Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Asset Values in the DU Underwriting Findings Report
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Non-Occupant Borrower Asset Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Liquid Assets
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: DU analyzes the value of liquid assets entered in the loan application in its risk assessment. Assets may be excluded from the loan application if the borrower can qualify without them.
elements.label: text

elements.text: DU considers the following assets liquid assets: Bonds, Bridge Loan Proceeds, Cash-on-Hand (for certain HomeReady loans only), Cash Value of Life Insurance, Certificate of Deposit, Checking, Gift (not deposited), Gift of Equity, Grant (not deposited), Individual Development Account, Money Market, Mutual Fund, Net Equity, Other Liquid Asset, Proceeds from Real Estate Property to be sold on or before closing, Proceeds from Sale of Non-Real Estate Asset, Retirement, Savings, Secured Borrowed Funds, Stocks, Stock Options (vested), and Trust Account.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: Some of these asset types may not be available in the lender's loan origination system.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Non-Liquid Assets
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: DU does not consider the amount of non-liquid assets. Non-liquid assets do not have to be verified, and will not be identified in a verification message.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: DU considers the following assets non-liquid assets: Cash Deposit on Sales, Net Worth of Business, Other NonLiquid Asset, and Unsecured Borrowed Funds.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: Some of these asset types may not be available in the lender's loan origination system.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Reserve Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: For loan casefiles underwritten with DU, DU will determine the reserve requirements based on the overall risk assessment of the loan casefile and the minimum reserves that may be required for the transaction. Reserves may be considered a compensating factor in DU's risk analysis, and may serve to improve the underwriting recommendation.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Refer to the following topics for additional requirements related to minimum reserves:
elements.label: text

elements.text: B2-2-03, Multiple Financed Properties for the Same Borrower
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: B3-4.1-01, Minimum Reserve Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Asset Values in the DU Underwriting Findings Report
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The DU Underwriting Findings report will identify the following values:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Total Available Assets: the total of all borrower(s)' liquid assets entered into DU;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Funds Required to Close: includes the cash needed to complete the transaction plus any debts marked paid by closing on purchase or limited cash-out refinance transactions, other than subject property mortgage(s);
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Reserves Required to be Verified: the amount of reserves that must be verified;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Total Funds to be Verified: the sum of Funds Required to Close and Reserves Required to be Verified; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Excess Available Assets, not required to be verified by DU: liquid assets that DU is not requiring the lender to verify.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The Excess Available Assets, not required to be verified by DU (Excess Available Assets) amount represents the amount of assets remaining after subtracting the Total Funds to be Verified from the Total Available Assets. Excess Available Assets do not generally need to be verified.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Non-Occupant Borrower Asset Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Assets that are owned by a non-occupant borrower can be included in the 5% minimum borrower contribution requirement (when applicable), and those funds must be entered in the loan application. Total liquid assets for the occupying borrower and non-occupant borrower are included in DU's calculation of total available assets.
elements.label: text

elements.text: B3-4.4-02, Requirements for Certain Assets in DU (06/01/2022)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information about certain asset policies in DU, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Asset Verification Documentation
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Depository Assets
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Bridge Loan
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Cash Deposit on Sales Contract (Earnest Money)
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Gifts and Grants
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Gifts of Equity
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Net Equity from Properties Pending Sale
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Proceeds from Sold Properties
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Other Liquid Assets
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Secured Borrowed Funds
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Asset Verification Documentation
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When DU requires assets to be verified, DU will indicate the minimum verification documentation requirements necessary for the lender to process the loan application. This level of documentation may not be adequate for every borrower and every situation. The lender must determine whether additional documentation is warranted.
elements.label: text

elements.text: DU will not require documentation of assets for refinance transactions when the total funds to be verified are $500 or less.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Depository Assets
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: For depository assets (checking and savings accounts, money market funds, and certificates of deposit), DU will require the following:
elements.label: text

elements.text: two consecutive monthly bank statements (60 days of account activity) for all purchase transactions, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: one monthly statement (30 days of account activity) for all limited cash-out and cash-out refinance transactions.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Monthly bank statements must be dated within 45 days of the initial loan application date.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Quarterly bank statements must be dated within 90 days of the initial loan application date, and the lender must confirm that the funds in the account have not been transferred to another asset account that is verified with more current documentation.
elements.label: text

elements.text: A Verification of Deposit (Form 1006) can be obtained in place of bank statements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When DU validates assets, DU issues a message indicating the acceptable documentation. Compliance with the DU message satisfies the requirement for documenting assets. This documentation may differ from the requirements described above. See  B3-2-02, DU Validation Service.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Bridge Loan
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Enter the amount of a bridge (or swing) loan in the asset section 2a of the loan application. Do not include the amount of the bridge loan in any other liquid asset. (For example, do not enter the amount of the loan both as a bridge loan and in a checking account, even if the loan funds have been deposited.)
elements.label: text

elements.text: Bridge loans should also be considered in the net equity calculation for properties that are pending sale. (In other words, the amount of the bridge loan should be subtracted from the net proceeds to avoid counting this asset twice.)
elements.label: text

elements.text: See  B3-4.3-14, Bridge/Swing Loans for additional information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : It may also be necessary to enter the bridge loan and corresponding monthly payment as a recurring liability in the liabilities section of the loan application. See the bridge loan liability discussion in  B3-6-05, Monthly Debt Obligations.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Cash Deposit on Sales Contract (Earnest Money)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Earnest money is entered in Section 2b in the loan application. It is treated as a credit to the transaction and will reduce the borrower's required funds to close as reflected in Section L4. The earnest money check is assumed to have cleared the borrower's bank account and must not be reflected in the balance of an asset account.
elements.label: text

elements.text: See  B3-4.3-09, Earnest Money Deposit, for documentation requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Gifts and Grants
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Gifts or donations from entities (grants) are permitted and documented in accordance with B3-4.3-04, Personal Gifts, and  B3-4.3-06, Grants and Lender Contributions.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Gifts and grants are entered in Section 4d of the loan application and identified as being deposited or not deposited. Gifts and grants that are deposited have been received by the borrower and the value should be included in another asset account. The amount of the gift or grant is not included in available funds.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Gifts and grants that are not deposited are not included in another asset account. The amount of the gift is included in available funds.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Gifts of Equity
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Enter a gift of equity in Section 4d of the loan application.
elements.label: text

elements.text: A gift of equity must meet the gift of equity requirements defined in  B3-4.3-05, Gifts of Equity.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Net Equity from Properties Pending Sale
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: See  B3-4.3-10, Anticipated Sales Proceeds, for additional information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender must calculate the net equity outside of DU for a property marked Pending Sale in Section 3 of the loan application. The amount is entered in the asset section (2b) using the asset type Proceeds from Real Estate Property to be Sold on or Before Closing.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If a bridge loan is obtained, the amount of the bridge loan is entered as an asset and must be subtracted from net equity before entry in the loan application.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When the net equity is positive, DU will add the amount to the funds available for closing. When the net equity is negative, DU will subtract the amount from the funds available for closing.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Proceeds from Sold Properties
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Proceeds from properties that have already been sold must be included in a depository account, such as a checking or savings account.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Other Liquid Assets
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Enter the value of personal assets that will be converted to a liquid asset (or sold) prior to closing in Section 2b using the asset type Proceeds from Sale of Non-Real Estate Asset. (See B3-4.3-18, Sale of Personal Assets, for additional information.) Life insurance that will be used for the transaction is entered in Section 2a using the account type Cash Value of Life Insurance. An Other Asset (liquid) may be entered for other types of assets that will be used for the transaction, such as pending tax refunds that will be received prior to closing.
elements.label: text

elements.text: A verification message will require evidence of the value of the asset and confirmation that the asset was converted to cash.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Secured Borrowed Funds
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Borrowers can borrow against an asset they own, such as a 401(k) account or real estate, according to the requirements of  B3-6-05, Monthly Debt Obligations. The amount of the secured loan should be entered in Section 2b using the asset type Secured Borrowed Funds. The secured loan amount should be subtracted from the market value of the actual asset, and the net asset value should be entered. For example, if the borrower has a vested value, less taxes and penalties, of $30,000 in a 401(k) account and borrows $10,000 against the 401(k), enter $10,000 as secured borrowed funds and enter $20,000 as retirement funds.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: A loan that is secured against a liquid asset owned by the borrower (such as a 401(k) or mutual fund) does not have to be entered as a liability in the loan application if the appropriate documentation is provided.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Loans that are secured against real estate, or any other non-liquid asset, must be entered as the applicable liability (for example, as a mortgage).
elements.label: text

elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Delivery of Credit Scores
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Loan-Level Price Adjustments Based on Credit Score
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Credit Score Versions
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Credit scores are required for most loans purchased or securitized by Fannie Mae. Fannie Mae is in the process of implementing new credit score models to support transition to a modernized and more competitive credit score framework for loan underwriting. The credit score models approved by U.S. Federal Housing (FHFA) are classic FICO, VantageScore® 4.0, and FICO 10T.
elements.label: text

elements.text: As Fannie Mae advances implementation of this modernized framework, the following versions of the classic FICO score, produced from software developed by Fair Isaac Corporation, are currently required for loans underwritten through DU and manually underwritten mortgage loans:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Equifax Beacon® 5.0;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Experian®/Fair Isaac Risk Model V2SM; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: TransUnion FICO® Risk Score, Classic 04.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The lender must request these FICO credit scores for each borrower from each of the Fannie Mae-approved score providers when they order the three in-file merged credit report. If the borrower's credit file includes complete and accurate information sufficient to ensure the validity of the credit score, the lender does not need to further evaluate the borrower's creditworthiness.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The following versions of VantageScore 4.0 are eligible for use by approved lenders:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Equifax VantageScore 4.0,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Experian VantageScore 4.0,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: TransUnion VantageScore 4.0.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Lenders that wish to participate in the delivery of loans assessed using VantageScore 4.0 should contact their Fannie Mae customer account team for information regarding program availability, eligibility requirements, and applicable delivery instructions.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : The credit report will indicate if a credit score could not be produced due to insufficient credit. The credit report must be maintained in the loan file, whether the report includes traditional credit and a credit score or indicates that a credit score could not be produced due to insufficient or frozen credit.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Consistent with FHFA approvals supporting credit score modernization, the following models are also approved for use in connection with future loans delivered to Fannie Mae.
elements.label: text

elements.text: FICO 10T Versions
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: FICO Score 10T (Equifax, Experian, TransUnion)
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Fannie Mae is working to implement FICO Score 10T in a timely manner. Lenders will be advised when loans with FICO Score 10T can be delivered.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: DU Credit Score Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Credit scores are not an integral part of DU's risk assessment because DU performs its own analysis of the credit report data. However, lenders must request credit scores for each borrower from each of the three credit repositories when they order the three in-file merged credit report, described in B3-5.2-01, Requirements for Credit Reports. If one or two of the credit repositories do not contain any credit information for the borrowers who have traditional credit, the credit report is still acceptable as long as
elements.label: text

elements.label: list_item

elements.label: list_item

elements.label: text

elements.text: Frozen Credit Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: If the borrower's credit information is frozen at one of the credit repositories for borrowers who have traditional credit, the credit report is still acceptable as long as
elements.label: text

elements.text: credit data is available from two repositories, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the lender requested a three in-file merged report.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Loans for borrowers with credit data frozen at two or more of the credit repositories will not be eligible whether underwritten manually or in DU.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Delivery of Credit Scores
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Loan Delivery collects credit score data for each borrower and also at the loan level. Lenders are required to deliver the representative credit score for all loans. For additional information, see the Loan Delivery Job Aid: Credit Scores.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Loan-Level Price Adjustments Based on Credit Score
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Loan-level price adjustments (LLPAs) are assessed based on the representative credit score for the loan, in addition to other eligibility and loan features. See the Loan-Level Price Adjustment (LLPA) Matrix for additional information about LLPAs, including information about how LLPAs are assessed for loans that include borrowers without a credit score.

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.label: text

elements.text: Representative Credit Score
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The representative credit score for the loan is determined based on the credit scores of each borrower and is used to determine loan eligibility for manually underwritten loans with only one borrower and for pricing purposes (i.e., assessing LLPAs) on all loans. Follow these steps to calculate the representative credit score for a loan:

elements.label: text

elements.label: text

elements.text: Description
elements.label: text

elements.text: Select a single score for each borrower.
elements.label: text

elements.text: • When two credit scores are obtained, choose the lower score.
elements.label: text

elements.text: • When three credit scores are obtained, choose the middle score. (If two of the three scores are
elements.label: text

elements.text: the same, choose the middle of the three scores. For example: 700, 680, 680 = 680; 700, 700,
elements.label: text

elements.text: If there is only one borrower, the single score for the borrower is the representative credit score
elements.label: text

elements.text: for the loan.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If there are multiple borrowers, determine the applicable credit score for each individual
elements.label: text

elements.text: borrower and select the lowest applicable score from the group as the representative credit
elements.label: text

elements.text: score for the loan. If there is a borrower who does not have a credit score, determine the
elements.label: text

elements.text: representative credit score for the loan based on the credit scores of the other borrowers on the
elements.label: text

elements.text: loan.

elements.text: Foreign Credit Reports and Credit Scores
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: With the exception of loan casefiles underwritten through DU, Fannie Mae permits the lender to use a credit report from a foreign country to document a borrower's credit history. (See B3-5.2-01, Requirements for Credit Reports.) If a credit score is provided with the foreign credit report it cannot be used to establish eligibility, or be delivered to Fannie Mae unless the credit score is an approved credit score model, as required by B3-5.1-01, General Requirements for Credit Scores.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: If the lender relies on credit reports from foreign countries to document borrower credit histories, the credit report must meet the requirements and standards for domestic reports and must be completed in English or include an English translation.
elements.label: text

elements.text: See Section B3-5.4, Nontraditional Credit History, for requirements that apply when a loan includes a borrower without an acceptable credit history.

elements.text: Section B3-5.2, Credit Reports
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: B3-5.2-01, Requirements for Credit Reports (11/05/2025)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic describes requirements for credit reports, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Overview
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: General Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Public Records Information
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Acceptable and Unacceptable Changes
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Required Creditor Information
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Format for Reporting Payment History
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Inquiries
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Unreported Debts
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Assessing Borrower Credit Management Skills
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Credit Report Requirements in Desktop Underwriter
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Credit Score Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Overview
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender must obtain a credit report for each borrower on the loan application who has an individual credit record. The credit report must be based on data provided by the national credit repositories. Acceptable credit report formats are described in  B3-5.2-02, Types of Credit Reports. For credit report requirements in DU see below.
elements.label: text

elements.text: A nontraditional mortgage credit report or other form of alternative credit verification may be used if the borrower
elements.label: text

elements.text: does not have sufficient credit to enable the development of a credit score, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: does not use the type of credit that is reported to credit repositories.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: See Section B3-5.4, Nontraditional Credit History for additional information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: General Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Credit reports must meet the following general requirements:
elements.label: text

elements.text: The report must include both credit and public record information for each borrower.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The report must include all discovered credit and legal information that is not considered obsolete under the Fair Credit Reporting Act. Although the Fair Credit Reporting Act currently specifies that credit information is not considered obsolete until after seven years, and bankruptcy information after ten years, Fannie Mae requires only a seven-year history to be reviewed for all credit and public record information.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The report must be an original report, with no erasures, white-outs, or alterations. An automated credit report or one that is transmitted by fax is considered to be an 'original' report.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The report must include the full name, address, and telephone number of the credit reporting agency, as well as the names of the national repositories that the agency used to provide information for the report.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The credit reporting agency must make responsive statements about all items on the credit report-indicating 'unable to verify' or 'employer refused to verify,' when appropriate.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Public Records Information
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The report must include all available public records information, identify the sources of the public records information, and disclose whether any judgments, foreclosures, tax liens, or bankruptcies were discovered (with these adverse items reported in accordance with the Fair Credit Reporting Act and to the extent reported by consumer reporting agencies participating in the National Consumer Assistance Plan).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Acceptable and Unacceptable Changes
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Collected credit report information should not be changed. However, it is permissible to delete duplicate information, translate codes to plain language, and make appropriate adjustments to resolve conflicting information to ensure the clarity of the report.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The following types of changes are unacceptable:
elements.label: text

elements.text: deleting tradelines that pertain to a borrower's bankruptcy,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: adding a payment amount to a creditor's tradeline when the creditor does not require a payment, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: restricting information collection to a shorter time period than Fannie Mae requires.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Credit repositories should only change the information called to its attention by a creditor or a party that is not associated with either the real estate sale or purchase transaction or the mortgage financing.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Required Creditor Information
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: For each debt listed, the report must provide:
elements.label: text

elements.text: the creditor's name,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the date the account was opened,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the amount of the highest credit,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the current status of the account,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the required payment amount,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the unpaid balance, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a payment history.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The report must indicate the dates that accounts were last updated with the creditors. Each account with a balance must have been checked with the creditor within 90 days of the date of the credit report.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Format for Reporting Payment History
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: All data must be presented in a format that is easy to read and that is understandable without the need for code translations.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The report must list the historical status of each account. This status must be presented in a 'number of times past due' format and include the dates of the delinquencies.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The preferred format is '0 x 30, 0 x 60, 0 x 90 days' late. The following formats are also acceptable:
elements.label: text

elements.text: 'Rl, R2, R3, …,' if it also gives historical negative ratings, such as 'was R3 in 6/05.'
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a consecutive numbering sequence, such as '0001000 …,' provided the meaning is clear from the report.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Statements such as 'current,' 'satisfactory,' or 'as agreed' are not satisfactory by themselves.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Inquiries
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: The report must generate an inquiry that will appear on future credit reports and must list all inquiries that were made in the previous 90 days.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Unreported Debts
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: If the credit report does not include a reference for each significant open debt on the application, the lender must obtain a separate written verification for each unreported debt. The lender also needs to verify separately accounts listed as 'will rate by mail only' or 'need written authorization.'
elements.label: text

elements.text: Assessing Borrower Credit Management Skills
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The borrower's credit management skills can be assessed by analyzing repayment patterns, credit utilization, and level of experience in using credit.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Credit Report Requirements in Desktop Underwriter
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders are required to request a three in-file merged credit report from one of the credit information providers listed on Fannie Mae's website. The credit report used by DU in the final loan casefile submission must be maintained in the mortgage loan file. A DU observation message will identify all of the credit reports evaluated by DU during the loan submission. The version of the credit report received by DU must meet the requirements in this topic and be one that supports trended credit data. Trended credit data is expanded information on a borrower's credit history at a tradeline level on several monthly factors, including: amount owed, minimum payment, and payment made.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : To obtain a credit report that is compatible with DU loan casefile requirements, the borrower's present address must be within the U.S. or U.S. territories, with the exception of an Army Post Office (APO), Fleet Post Office (FPO), or Diplomatic Post Office (DPO) military address. Borrowers with foreign credit reports must be manually underwritten. See Foreign Credit Reports and Credit Scores in B3-5.1-02, Determining the Credit Score for a Mortgage Loan for additional information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Credit Score Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: See B3-5.1-01, General Requirements for Credit Scores, for additional information about credit report requirements related to credit scores.

elements.text: B3-5.2-02, Types of Credit Reports (11/05/2025)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on the types of credit reports that are accepted by Fannie Mae, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: In-File Credit Reports
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Automated Merged Credit Reports
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Residential Mortgage Credit Reports
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: In-File Credit Reports
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: An in-file credit report provides credit and public record information obtained from one or more credit repositories. The report contains 'as is' information, which typically has not been updated or re-verified as a result of the credit inquiry.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The report must meet the following requirements:
elements.label: text

elements.text: The report should include all information from three different credit repositories, or two repositories, if: that is the extent of the data available for the borrower, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the borrower's credit information is frozen at one credit repository.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If only one in-file credit report is available for a borrower, this is acceptable provided the lender requested information from three credit repositories.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If the report does not include a reference for each significant debt reported by the borrower on the loan application, the lender must obtain a separate written verification for each unreported (or unrated) debt.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If the report lists accounts that were not checked with the creditor within 90 days of the date of the in-file report, the lender must obtain an updated credit report or a separate written verification for those accounts.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Automated Merged Credit Reports
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: An automated merged credit report combines the in-file credit reports from multiple repositories into a single report. A joint merged credit report includes all credit repository credit data on more than one individual applicant.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: The report must meet the following requirements:
elements.label: text

elements.text: The report must include all information from three different credit repositories, or two repositories, if:
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: that is the extent of the data available for the borrower, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the borrower's credit information is frozen at one credit repository.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If information from only one credit repository is available, this is acceptable provided the lender requested information from three different credit repositories.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The report cannot be provided by a credit reporting agency that is affiliated with the lender in any way.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The report must include all information reported for the borrower from the in-file credit reports.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The report must identify the repositories that were used for the in-file credit reports.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The report does not have to repeat duplicate information that is in in-file credit reports. However, if duplicate information is not exactly the same on each report, the automated merged report must either repeat the information or include the most derogatory of the duplicate information that pertains to payment history and/or current payment status.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Residential Mortgage Credit Reports
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A residential mortgage credit report is a detailed account of the borrower's credit, employment, and residency history, as well as public records information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The report must meet the following requirements:
elements.label: text

elements.text: The credit reporting agency must contact at least two national repositories of accumulated credit records for each locality in which the borrower has lived during the most recent two-year period.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: All information must be obtained from, or verified by, sources other than the borrower. When coborrowers have individually obtained credit, separate repository inquiries are necessary, although the results of both reports may be combined in one report, as long as the report clearly indicates that this has been done.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The credit reporting agency must verify, either in writing or by telephone, the borrower's current employment and income (if it can be obtained). If the borrower has changed jobs in the past two years, the credit report also must mention the borrower's previous employment and income.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The report must include a positive statement that the employment was verified, the date of the verification, and the name of the individual who confirmed the employment. If this information was not obtained by an employer interview, the credit reporting agency must indicate why that was not done.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The report must include the name of the party who ordered the report. If another party paid for the report, the credit report must provide that party's name, unless the lender ordered the report and the billed party has a documented agent or corporate relationship with the lender.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The original report must be delivered to the office of the party who requested it, using any means acceptable under the Fair Credit Reporting Act or other similar regulations, such as sending it through the U.S. postal system, by messenger, over a fax machine, or through other automated means.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The report must include a certification that it meets the standards for a residential mortgage credit report.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: When the credit reporting agency has incomplete information, discovers that the borrower might not have disclosed all information that should be found in the public records, or obtains other information that indicates the possible existence of undisclosed credit records, the credit reporting agency must interview the borrower(s)
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: to obtain additional information that is needed to provide an accurate report or perform additional research to verify whether the purported undisclosed records actually exist.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Announcements

elements.text: B3-5.2-03, Accuracy of Credit Information in a Credit Report (07/25/2017)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on the following:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Accuracy of Credit Information in a Credit Report
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Disputed Tradelines
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Accuracy of Credit Information in a Credit Report
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: For all mortgage loans (including DU loan casefiles and manually underwritten loans), the lender is responsible for reviewing the credit report, as well as all credit information, to determine that the credit report meets Fannie Mae's requirements and that the data evaluated by DU was accurate.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If a borrower indicates that any significant information in the credit file is inaccurate-such as reported accounts that do not belong to the borrower or derogatory information that is reported in error-the lender should carefully review the credit information with the borrower, then request the credit reporting company that provided the information to confirm its accuracy.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Disputed Tradelines
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Manually Underwritten Loans
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: If the borrower has disputed information in their credit file, and the credit reporting company confirms that the disputed information is incorrect or incomplete and underwriting the loan needs to be completed before the credit files can be corrected, the lender cannot use the credit score(s) when manually underwriting the loan. Instead, the credit risk assessment must be based on a review of the borrower's traditional credit history.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If there are multiple disputed tradelines or a dispute on a mortgage tradeline, the lender should obtain correspondence directly from the borrower indicating the reason for the dispute. The aspect of the tradeline-such as balance and payment history-that is being disputed is of particular interest when considering the impact to the borrower's overall credit profile.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: The lender is responsible for determining whether the borrower's explanation is reasonable and/or whether additional documentation (such as canceled checks) is necessary to disprove the adverse information. Lenders are not required to investigate disputed medical tradelines.
elements.label: text

elements.text: DU Loans
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: For loan casefiles underwritten through DU, DU will indicate if the lender is required to investigate the disputed account to determine if the account belongs to the borrower and confirm the accuracy and completeness of the information reported on the account.
elements.label: text

elements.text: See B3-2-09, Erroneous Credit Report Data; B3-2-10, Accuracy of DU Data, DU Tolerances, and Errors in the Credit Report; and B3-5.3-09, DU Credit Report Analysis for additional information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Recent Related Announcements
elements.label: text

elements.text: There are no recently issued Announcements related to this topic.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Section B3-5.3, Traditional Credit History
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: B3-5.3-01, Number and Age of Accounts (04/01/2009)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on the number and age of accounts.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Number and Age of Accounts
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Number and Age of Accounts
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender must review the borrower's credit report to determine whether they have an older established credit history or a newly established credit history, and whether there are a significant number of recently opened accounts or a mix of new accounts and older accounts.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Credit histories that include older, established accounts generally represent lower credit risk. However, an older, established credit history that includes a significant number of recently opened accounts may indicate that the borrower is overextended, and thus will represent a higher credit risk.
elements.label: text

elements.text: A newly established credit history does not automatically represent a higher credit risk, since making payments
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: as agreed on newly opened accounts represents less of a risk than not making payments as agreed on older, established accounts.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Recent Related Announcements
elements.label: text

elements.text: There are no recently issued Announcements related to this topic.
elements.label: text

elements.text: B3-5.3-02, Payment History (04/01/2009)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on the borrower's payment history.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Payment History
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Payment History
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender must review the borrower's credit report to determine the current status of each credit account (including mortgage accounts), the timeliness of payments, and the frequency, recency, and severity of any delinquent payments.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Credit histories that include no late payments, collection or charged-off accounts, foreclosures, deeds-inlieu, bankruptcies, or other public records information represent a lower credit risk.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Credit histories that include recent late payments represent a higher credit risk than those with late payments that occurred more than 24 months ago. When there are payments that were 30, 60, or 90 days (or longer) past due, the lender must determine whether the late payments represent isolated incidences or frequent occurrences. Delinquent payments must be evaluated in the context of the borrower's overall credit history, including the number and age of accounts, credit utilization, and recent attempts to obtain new credit. For example, a credit history that includes delinquent payments along with recent inquiries and a high balances-to-limits ratio indicates a high credit risk.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Credit histories that include foreclosures, deeds-in-lieu, and public records information (such as bankruptcies, judgments, and liens) represent a higher credit risk. The greater the number of such incidences and the more recently they occurred, the higher the credit risk.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: For information about mortgage payment history, see B3-5.3-03, Previous Mortgage Payment History.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Recent Related Announcements
elements.label: text

elements.text: There are no recently issued Announcements related to this topic.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: B3-5.3-03, Previous Mortgage Payment History (07/25/2017)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on previous mortgage payment history requirements, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Documenting Previous Mortgage History
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Standard Mortgage Verifications from Servicers
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Existing Mortgage Payment Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Excessive Mortgage Delinquency
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Documenting Previous Mortgage History
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender must review the borrower's credit report to determine the status of all mortgage accounts. If a borrower had previous mortgages, the lender does not have to independently verify the mortgage's payment history provided the credit report includes a reference to the mortgage (or mortgages) and reflects 12 months of the most recent payment activity.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If adequate mortgage payment history is not included in the borrower's credit report, the lender must use the following to verify the borrower's payment history on a previous mortgage(s):
elements.label: text

elements.text: a standard mortgage verification;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: loan payment history from the servicer;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the borrower's canceled checks for the last 12 months; or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the borrower's year-end mortgage account statement, provided the statement includes a payment receipt history, and, if applicable, canceled checks for the months elapsed since the year-end mortgage account statement was issued.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Standard Mortgage Verifications from Servicers
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When a lender relies on standard mortgage verifications from servicers or holders, it must ensure that the verifications include:
elements.label: text

elements.text: the unpaid principal balance of the mortgage and monthly payment amount;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the present status of the mortgage, such as current, 30 days' delinquent, etc.; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the borrower's payment history.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: When a servicer fails to provide all of the requested information, the lender must rely on information provided through the borrower's canceled checks. The checks must:
elements.label: text

elements.text: be legible,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: identify the mortgage servicer or mortgage holder as the payee,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: indicate that the servicer or holder endorsed the check for deposit, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: indicate the date the servicer or holder deposited the check.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Existing Mortgage Payment Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: On the date of the loan application, the borrower's existing mortgage must be current, which means that no more than 45 days may have elapsed since the last paid installment date.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Excessive Mortgage Delinquency
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender must review the borrower's credit history to determine previous mortgage delinquency, severity (e.g., 30, 60, or 90 days), and recency of the delinquency. Loans with excessive prior mortgage delinquencies are not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae. Excessive prior mortgage delinquency is defined as any mortgage tradeline that has one or more 60-, 90-, 120-, or 150-day delinquency reported within the 12 months prior to the credit report date. See B3-5.3-02, Payment History, and B3-5.3-07, Significant Derogatory Credit Events Waiting Periods and Re-establishing Credit for additional information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : For purposes of complying with the guidelines in this topic, timeshare accounts identified as mortgage tradelines are not required to meet the requirements described above, and are considered to be installment accounts.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Recent Related Announcements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: There are no recently issued Announcements related to this topic.
elements.label: text

elements.text: B3-5.3-04, Inquiries: Recent Attempts to Obtain New Credit (04/01/2009)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on inquiries: recent attempts to obtain new credit.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Inquiries: Recent Attempts to Obtain New Credit
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Inquiries: Recent Attempts to Obtain New Credit
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender must review the section of the borrower's credit report that indicates the presence of creditor inquiries to determine the number and recency of the inquiries.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Recent inquiries may indicate that the borrower has been actively seeking new credit accounts. The presence of a large number of unrelated inquiries represents higher credit risk (whether or not the borrower actually obtained credit as a result of the inquiry). The presence of many recent inquiries in combination with a significant number of recently opened accounts or delinquent accounts represents a high credit risk.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: When the credit report indicates that recent inquiries took place, the lender must confirm that the borrower has not obtained any additional credit that is not reflected in the credit report or the mortgage application. If additional credit was obtained, a verification of that debt must be provided and the borrower must be qualified with the monthly payment.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Recent Related Announcements
elements.label: text

elements.text: There are no recently issued Announcements related to this topic.
elements.label: text

elements.text: B3-5.3-05, Credit Utilization (05/31/2016)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on credit utilization.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Credit Utilization
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Credit Utilization
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When manually underwriting a loan, the lender must review the borrower's credit report to evaluate their use of revolving credit by comparing the current balance on each open account to the amount of credit that is available to determine whether the borrower has a pattern of using revolving accounts up to (or approaching) the credit limit. Patterns of revolving credit spending are credit risk indicative.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Credit histories that include revolving accounts with a low balances-to-limits ratio generally represent a lower credit risk, while those that include accounts with a high balances-to-limits ratio represent a higher credit risk.
elements.label: text

elements.text: A credit history that includes recently opened accounts that are at or near their limits may indicate that the borrower is overextended or overly reliant on the use of revolving credit-and, when this is combined with a delinquent payment history, it is generally an indication that the borrower has not managed their credit successfully.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : Lenders are not required to analyze trended credit data in the credit report. See B3-5.2-01, Requirements for Credit Reports, for additional information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Recent Related Announcements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: There are no recently issued Announcements related to this topic.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: B3-5.3-06, Authorized Users of Credit (10/30/2009)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on authorized users of credit
elements.label: text

elements.text: Authorized Users of Credit
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Consideration of Authorized User Accounts
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Authorized Users of Credit
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When a credit account owner permits another person, typically a family member who is managing credit for the first time, to have access to and use an account, the user is referred to as an authorized user of the account. This practice is intended to assist related individuals in legitimately establishing a credit history and credit score based on the account and payment history of the account owner, even though the authorized user is not the account owner.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Consideration of Authorized User Accounts
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: For manually underwritten loans, credit report tradelines that list a borrower as an authorized user cannot be considered in the underwriting decision, except as outlined below.
elements.label: text

elements.text: An authorized user tradeline may be considered if:
elements.label: text

elements.text: another borrower in the mortgage transaction is the owner of the tradeline; or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the borrower can provide written documentation (e.g., canceled checks, payment receipts, etc.) that they have been the actual and sole payer of the monthly payment on the account for at least 12 months preceding the date of the application.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If written documentation of the borrower's monthly payments on the authorized user tradeline is provided, then the payment history - particularly any late payments that are indicated - must be considered in the credit analysis and the monthly payment obligation must be included in the debt-to-income ratio.
elements.label: text

elements.text: An authorized user tradeline must be considered if the owner of the tradeline is the borrower's spouse and the spouse is not a borrower in the mortgage transaction.
elements.label: text

elements.text: These requirements do not apply to loan casefiles underwritten through DU. For DU requirements, see B3-5.3-09, DU Credit Report Analysis.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Recent Related Announcements
elements.label: text

elements.text: There are no recently issued Announcements related to this topic.
elements.label: text

elements.text: B3-5.3-07, Significant Derogatory Credit Events - Waiting Periods and Re-establishing Credit (08/07/2019)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on the waiting periods for significant derogatory credit events, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: General Information
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Identification of Significant Derogatory Credit Events in the Credit Report
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Bankruptcy (Chapter 7 or Chapter 11)
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Bankruptcy (Chapter 13)
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Multiple Bankruptcy Filings
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Foreclosure
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Foreclosure and Bankruptcy on the Same Mortgage
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Deed-in-Lieu of Foreclosure, Preforeclosure Sale, and Charge-Off of a Mortgage Account
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Summary - All Waiting Period Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Requirements for Re-establishing Credit
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: General Information
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The presence of significant derogatory credit events dramatically increases the likelihood of a future default and represents a significantly higher level of default risk. Examples of significant derogatory credit events include bankruptcies, foreclosures, deeds-in-lieu of foreclosure, preforeclosure sales, short sales, and charge-offs of mortgage accounts.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: The terms 'preforeclosure sale' and 'short sale' are used interchangeably in this Guide and have the same meaning (see Deed-in-Lieu of Foreclosure, Preforeclosure Sale, and Charge-Off of a Mortgage Account below).
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender must determine the cause and significance of the derogatory information, verify that sufficient time has elapsed since the date of the last derogatory information, and confirm that the borrower has re-established an acceptable credit history. The lender must make the final decision about the acceptability of a borrower's credit history when significant derogatory credit information exists.
elements.label: text

elements.text: This topic describes the amount of time that must elapse (the 'waiting period') after a significant derogatory credit event before the borrower is eligible for a new loan salable to Fannie Mae. The waiting period commences on the completion, discharge, or dismissal date (as applicable) of the derogatory credit event and ends on the disbursement date of the new loan for manually underwritten loans. See B3-5.3-09, DU Credit Report Analysis, for additional information pertaining to DU loan casefiles, including how the waiting period is determined. Also see B3-5.3-08, Extenuating Circumstances for Derogatory Credit, for additional information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: The requirements pertaining to significant derogatory credit are not applicable to high LTV refinance loans. (See B5-7-02, High LTV Refinance Underwriting, Documentation, and Collateral Requirements for the New Loan.)
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Identification of Significant Derogatory Credit Events in the Credit Report
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders must review the credit report and the Declarations in the loan application to identify instances of significant derogatory credit events. Lenders must review the public records section of the credit report and all tradelines, including mortgage accounts (first liens, second liens, home improvement loans, HELOCs, and manufactured home loans), to identify previous foreclosures, deeds-in-lieu, preforeclosure sales, charge-offs of mortgage accounts, and bankruptcies. Lenders must carefully review the current status of each tradeline, manner of payment codes, and remarks to identify these types of significant derogatory credit events. Remarks Codes are descriptive text or codes that appear on a tradeline, such as 'Foreclosure,' 'Forfeit deed-in-lieu of foreclosure,' and 'Settled for less than full balance.'
elements.label: text

elements.text: Significant derogatory credit events may not be accurately reported or consistently reported in the same manner by all creditors or credit reporting agencies. If not clearly identified in the credit report, the lender must obtain copies of appropriate documentation. The documentation must establish the completion date of a previous foreclosure, deed-in-lieu or preforeclosure sale, or date of the charge-off of a mortgage account; confirm the bankruptcy discharge or dismissal date; and identify debts that were not satisfied by the bankruptcy. Debts that were not satisfied by a bankruptcy must be paid off or have an acceptable, established repayment schedule.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: Timeshare accounts are considered installment loans and are not subject to the waiting periods described below.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Bankruptcy (Chapter 7 or Chapter 11)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A four-year waiting period is required, measured from the discharge or dismissal date of the bankruptcy action.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Exceptions for Extenuating Circumstances
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A two-year waiting period is permitted if extenuating circumstances can be documented, and is measured from the discharge or dismissal date of the bankruptcy action.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Bankruptcy (Chapter 13)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A distinction is made between Chapter 13 bankruptcies that were discharged and those that were dismissed. The waiting period required for Chapter 13 bankruptcy actions is measured as follows:
elements.label: text

elements.text: two years from the discharge date, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: four years from the dismissal date.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The shorter waiting period based on the discharge date recognizes that borrowers have already met a portion of the waiting period within the time needed for the successful completion of a Chapter 13 plan and subsequent discharge. A borrower who was unable to complete the Chapter 13 plan and received a dismissal will be held to a four-year waiting period.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Exceptions for Extenuating Circumstances
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: A two-year waiting period is permitted after a Chapter 13 dismissal, if extenuating circumstances can be documented. There are no exceptions permitted to the two-year waiting period after a Chapter 13 discharge.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Multiple Bankruptcy Filings
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: For a borrower with more than one bankruptcy filing within the past seven years, a five-year waiting period is required, measured from the most recent dismissal or discharge date.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : The presence of multiple bankruptcies in the borrower's credit history is evidence of significant derogatory credit and increases the likelihood of future default. Two or more borrowers with individual bankruptcies are not cumulative, and do not constitute multiple bankruptcies. For example, if the borrower has one bankruptcy and the co-borrower has one bankruptcy this is not considered a multiple bankruptcy.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Exceptions for Extenuating Circumstances
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A three-year waiting period is permitted if extenuating circumstances can be documented, and is measured from the most recent bankruptcy discharge or dismissal date. The most recent bankruptcy filing must have been the result of extenuating circumstances.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Foreclosure
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A seven-year waiting period is required, and is measured from the completion date of the foreclosure action as reported on the credit report or other foreclosure documents provided by the borrower.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Exceptions for Extenuating Circumstances
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A three-year waiting period is permitted if extenuating circumstances can be documented, and is measured from the completion date of the foreclosure action. Additional requirements apply between three and seven years, which include:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Maximum LTV, CLTV, or HCLTV ratios of the lesser of 90% or the maximum LTV, CLTV, or HCLTV ratios for the transaction per the Eligibility Matrix.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The purchase of a principal residence is permitted.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Limited cash-out refinances are permitted for all occupancy types pursuant to the eligibility requirements in effect at that time.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Note : The purchase of second homes or investment properties and cash-out refinances (any occupancy type) are not permitted until a seven-year waiting period has elapsed.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Foreclosure and Bankruptcy on the Same Mortgage
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: If a mortgage debt was discharged through a bankruptcy, the bankruptcy waiting periods may be applied if the lender obtains the appropriate documentation to verify that the mortgage obligation was discharged in the bankruptcy. Otherwise, the greater of the applicable bankruptcy or foreclosure waiting periods must be applied.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Deed-in-Lieu of Foreclosure, Preforeclosure Sale, and Charge-Off of a Mortgage Account
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: These transaction types are completed as alternatives to foreclosure.
elements.label: text

elements.text: A deed-in-lieu of foreclosure is a transaction in which the deed to the real property is transferred back to the servicer. These are typically identified on the credit report through Remarks Codes such as 'Forfeit deed-in-lieu of foreclosure.'
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: A preforeclosure sale or short sale is the sale of a property in lieu of a foreclosure resulting in a payoff of less than the total amount owed, which was pre-approved by the servicer. These are typically identified on the credit report through Remarks Codes such as 'Settled for less than full balance.'
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: A charge-off of a mortgage account occurs when a creditor has determined that there is little (or no) likelihood that the mortgage debt will be collected. A charge-off is typically reported after an account reaches a certain delinquency status, and is identified on the credit report with a manner of payment (MOP) code of '9.'
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: A four-year waiting period is required from the completion date of the deed-in-lieu of foreclosure, preforeclosure sale, or charge-off as reported on the credit report or other documents provided by the borrower.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Exceptions for Extenuating Circumstances
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A two-year waiting period is permitted if extenuating circumstances can be documented.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : Deeds-in-lieu and preforeclosure sales may not be accurately or consistently reported in the same manner by all creditors or credit reporting agencies. See Identification of Significant Derogatory Credit Events in the Credit Report above for additional information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Summary - All Waiting Period Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The following table summarizes the waiting period requirements for all significant derogatory credit events.
elements.label: text

elements.label: text

elements.text: Waiting Period
elements.label: text

elements.label: text

elements.label: text

elements.text: Requirements for Re-establishing Credit
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: After a bankruptcy, foreclosure, deed-in-lieu of foreclosure, preforeclosure sale, or charge-off of a mortgage account, the borrower's credit will be considered re-established if all of the following are met:
elements.label: text

elements.text: The waiting period and the related additional requirements are met.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The loan receives a recommendation from DU that is acceptable for delivery to Fannie Mae or, if manually underwritten, meets the minimum credit score requirements based on the parameters of the loan and the established eligibility requirements.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The borrower has traditional credit as outlined in Section B3-5.3, Traditional Credit History. Nontraditional credit or 'thin files' are not acceptable.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: When both a bankruptcy and foreclosure are disclosed on the loan application, or when both appear on the credit report, the lender may apply the bankruptcy waiting period if the lender obtains the appropriate documentation to verify that the mortgage loan in question was discharged in the bankruptcy. Otherwise, the greater of the applicable bankruptcy or foreclosure waiting period must be applied. 1
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: References to LTV ratios include LTV, CLTV, and HCLTV ratios. The maximum LTV ratios permitted are the lesser of the LTV ratios in this table or the maximum LTV ratios for the transaction per the Eligibility Matrix . 2
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: B3-5.3-08, Extenuating Circumstances for Derogatory Credit (12/16/2014)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic provides information on extenuating circumstances for derogatory credit information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Extenuating Circumstances
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Extenuating Circumstances
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Extenuating circumstances are nonrecurring events that are beyond the borrower's control that result in a sudden, significant, and prolonged reduction in income or a catastrophic increase in financial obligations.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If a borrower claims that derogatory information is the result of extenuating circumstances, the lender must substantiate the borrower's claim. Examples of documentation that can be used to support extenuating circumstances include
elements.label: text

elements.text: documents that confirm the event
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: such as a copy of a divorce decree, medical reports or bills, notice of job layoff, job severance papers, etc.; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: documents that illustrate factors that contributed to the borrower's inability to resolve the problems that resulted from the event
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: such as a copy of insurance papers or claim settlements, property listing agreements, lease agreements, tax returns (covering the periods prior to, during, and after a loss of employment), etc.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The lender must obtain a written explanation from the borrower explaining the relevance of the documentation. The written explanation must support the claims of extenuating circumstances, confirm the nature of the event that led to the bankruptcy or foreclosure-related action, and illustrate that the borrower had no reasonable options other than to default on his or her financial obligations. The written explanation may be in the form of a letter from the borrower, an email from the borrower, or some other form of written documentation provided by the borrower.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Recent Related Announcements There are no recently issued Announcements related to this topic.

elements.text: B3-5.3-09, DU Credit Report Analysis (06/03/2026)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic describes how DU analyzes credit report data and requirements lenders must follow in response to the credit-related Findings messages. This topic includes:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Inquiries
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Trended Credit Data
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Omitted Accounts
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Authorized User Tradelines
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Disputed Credit Report Tradelines
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: DU Debt Comparison
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Contradictory, Derogatory, or Erroneous Information
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Duplicate Public Records
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Judgments and Liens
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Mortgage Delinquencies
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Past-Due, Collection, and Charge-Off of Non-Mortgage Accounts
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Prior Bankruptcy, Foreclosure, Deed-in-Lieu of Foreclosure, Preforeclosure Sales, and Charge-Off of Mortgage Accounts
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Bankruptcy
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Foreclosure
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Deed-in-Lieu of Foreclosure
elements.label: list_item

elements.label: list_item
