elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Inquiries
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender should examine inquiries to determine whether they represent potential sources of undisclosed credit. If new debt was obtained, the lender may need to correct the loan application and resubmit it.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Trended Credit Data
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders are not required to analyze trended credit data in the credit report. For more information, see B3-2-03, Risk Factors Evaluated by DU.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Omitted Accounts
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Supporting documentation is required when a credit report liability with a balance greater than zero is omitted from the loan application.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Authorized User Tradelines
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: DU takes credit report tradelines designated as authorized user tradelines into consideration as part of the DU credit risk assessment. No additional investigation of the borrower's credit history is required by the lender unless instructed by DU. Refer to B3-6-05, Monthly Debt Obligations for requirements on when a debt may be excluded from a borrower's debt-to-income ratio.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For manual underwriting consideration of authorized users of credit, see B3-5.3-06, Authorized Users of Credit.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Disputed Credit Report Tradelines
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When the credit report contains tradelines disputed by the borrower, DU will first assess the risk of the loan casefile using all tradelines, including those disputed. If DU issues an Approve recommendation using the disputed tradelines, no further documentation or action is necessary. DU will issue a message specific to this scenario.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If DU does not issue an Approve recommendation when including the disputed tradelines, DU will re-assess the risk without using the disputed tradelines. If DU is then able to issue an Approve recommendation, the lender must investigate the tradelines to determine whether the borrower is responsible for the accounts or if the account information is accurate or complete.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the borrower is not responsible for the disputed accounts, the lender must obtain supporting documentation and may deliver the loan as a DU loan. No further action is necessary regarding the disputed tradelines.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If the borrower is responsible for the disputed account, the lender must investigate the information, including determining the aspect of the tradeline that is being disputed. If the borrower is able to provide documentation to disprove any adverse information (such as canceled checks), the lender may deliver the loan as a DU loan.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If the borrower is responsible for the disputed account and the account and tradeline information is accurate and complete, the loan is not eligible for delivery as a DU loan. The lender may manually underwrite the loan if the transaction is eligible for manual underwriting.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The monthly payments for the disputed tradelines must be included in the debt-to-income ratio if the accounts belong to the borrower.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : Tradelines reported as medical debt are not shown in the disputed tradeline message. Therefore, lenders are not required to investigate disputed medical tradelines.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Examples
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The following scenarios are examples of when a loan receiving an Approve/Eligible recommendation with the disputed tradeline(s) excluded from DU's risk assessment would be eligible for delivery as a DU loan:
elements.label: text

elements.text: A borrower's account was referred for collection by the creditor. Subsequently, the borrower paid off the account, but the pay-off was not reported on the tradeline. The borrower requested that a dispute be placed on the tradeline. The tradeline information was accurate, but because it did not reflect that the borrower paid off the account, it may be considered incomplete. The borrower must provide documentation that the account was paid in full.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: A borrower and his son have the same name (Sr. and Jr.). The borrower's credit report contains a tradeline that actually belongs to the son. The tradeline is reported as disputed. The borrower can provide confirmation that they are not obligated on the account.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The servicer of a disputed loan indicates a late payment in January of the previous year. The borrower can provide documentation (such as canceled checks or bank statements) that indicate that the payment was made on time.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The following scenario is an example of when a loan receiving an Approve/Eligible recommendation with the disputed tradeline(s) excluded from DU's risk assessment would not be eligible for delivery as a DU loan:
elements.label: text

elements.text: The credit report indicates a disputed tradeline on the borrower's mortgage being refinanced. The tradeline indicates a 60-day late payment in January of the previous year. The borrower cannot provide any documentation to support that the payment was made on time.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: DU Debt Comparison
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: DU compares the balances and payments of the debts on the credit report with the debts on the loan application. If material differences are found, the lender must confirm that all debts from the credit report are included on the loan application and provide documentation to support the use of payments and balances lower than those on the credit report. If the debt affects the debt-to-income ratio by more than the allowable tolerances, the lender must add the debt to the loan application and resubmit the loan. Otherwise, the lender is expected to provide documentation that supports the omission from the loan application. See B3-6-02, Debt-toIncome Ratios, and B3-2-10, Accuracy of DU Data, DU Tolerances, and Errors in the Credit Report, for additional information.)
elements.label: text

elements.text: Contradictory, Derogatory, or Erroneous Information
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders are obligated to take action when contradictory, derogatory, or erroneous information would justify additional investigation or would provide grounds for a decision that is different from the recommendation DU delivers. For example, if the credit report reflects a previous foreclosure but the information was not accurately mapped to DU, the lender must consider this when making its final underwriting decision.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Duplicate Public Records
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Items that typically appear in the Public Records section of the credit report (judgments, bankruptcies, foreclosures, and tax liens) are often duplicated because the credit agencies may not attempt to merge items of this severe nature. As a result, these items may also appear in more than one verification message in the Underwriting Findings report. If it is clear from the credit report data that the items are duplicates (identical account numbers, date filed, and dollar amounts), the lender can disregard the duplicates and document the item once. However, if it is unclear from the credit report whether any of the items are duplicated, the lender should treat each item individually and obtain the required documentation for each item, as indicated in the verification messages.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Judgments and Liens
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Open judgments and all outstanding liens that are in the Public Records section of the credit report will be identified in the Underwriting Findings report, and must be paid off at or prior to closing. Documentation of the satisfaction of these liabilities, along with verification of funds sufficient to satisfy these obligations, must also be maintained in the permanent loan file.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Mortgage Delinquencies
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: DU applies the following guidelines to the processing of loans with mortgage delinquencies:
elements.label: text

elements.text: If any borrower's credit report contains a mortgage tradeline that is 60 or more days past due when the account was last reported by the creditor and the account was reported within the 12 months prior to the credit report date, the loan casefile will receive an Ineligible recommendation and will be ineligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If there is a mortgage that is disclosed on the loan application but not reported on the credit report, or the mortgage is on the credit report with an outstanding balance but the payment history has not been reported in the last six months, DU will issue a message requiring the lender to confirm that the account is not two or more payments past due as of the date of the application and that it has not been past due by two or more payments in the last 12 months. If the lender determines that the borrower does have a mortgage that is past due by two or more payments or has been past due by two or more payments in the last 12 months, then the loan casefile is not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Borrowers may not bring past-due mortgage accounts current prior to closing in order to circumvent Fannie Mae's policy regarding past-due mortgages. However, the lender may apply some discretion with regard to the application of this policy if it determines and documents that the past-due account status was not the fault of the borrower-for example, if the servicer misapplied or lost the borrower's payment.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Loan casefiles will receive an Ineligible recommendation due to excessive prior mortgage delinquency if the borrower has a mortgage tradeline on their credit report that has one or more 60-, 90-, 120-, or 150day delinquency reported within the 12 months prior to the credit report date.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The above policies will apply to all mortgage tradelines, including first liens, second liens, home improvement loans, HELOCs, and manufactured home loans.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Underwriting when the Credit Report Contains Inaccurate Mortgage Delinquency Information
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When DU identifies a mortgage delinquency on the credit report and the information is inaccurate, the lender may instruct DU to disregard the mortgage delinquency information on the credit report. This is done by entering 'Confirmed Mtg Del Incorrect' in the online loan application and resubmitting the loan casefile to DU. When the loan casefile is resubmitted to DU, the mortgage delinquency information on the credit report will not be used.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If the lender enters 'Confirmed Mtg Del Incorrect', the lender must document that the mortgage is not currently 60 days or more past due, and has not been 60 days or more past due in the last 12 months.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Past-Due, Collection, and Charge-Off of Non-Mortgage Accounts
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Accounts that are reported as past due (not reported as collection accounts) must be brought current. Medical collection accounts are excluded from the limits below and are not required to be paid in full at or prior to closing.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For one-unit, principal residence properties, borrowers are not required to pay off outstanding collections
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: or non-mortgage charge-offs-regardless of the amount.
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Note : If the lender marks the collection account Paid By Close in the online loan application, DU will issue a message in the DU Underwriting Findings report stating that the collection must be paid.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For two- to four-unit owner-occupied and second home properties, collections and non-mortgage chargeoffs totaling more than $5,000 must be paid in full prior to or at closing.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: For investment properties, individual collection and non-mortgage charge-off accounts equal to or greater than $250 and accounts that total more than $1,000 must be paid in full prior to or at closing.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Prior Bankruptcy, Foreclosure, Deed-in-Lieu of Foreclosure, Preforeclosure Sales, and Charge-Off of Mortgage Accounts
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Per the requirements of B3-5.3-07, Significant Derogatory Credit Events - Waiting Periods and Re-establishing Credit, an amount of time must elapse (the 'waiting period') after a significant derogatory credit event before the borrower is eligible for a new loan salable to Fannie Mae. The waiting period commences on the completion, discharge, or dismissal date (as applicable) of the derogatory credit event and ends on the disbursement date of the new loan. Because DU does not have the disbursement date of the subject loan, DU uses the date of the credit report to measure whether or not the applicable waiting period has been met. However, because the credit report date may not result in an accurate calculation of the waiting period (it is earlier than the disbursement date), the lender may use the disbursement date to confirm that the waiting period has been met. See the table below for additional information.
elements.label: text

elements.label: text

elements.text: Bankruptcy
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: DU applies the following guidelines to prior bankruptcies:
elements.label: text

elements.text: If a Chapter 13 bankruptcy was discharged within the last two years, dismissed within the last four years, or filed but neither discharged nor dismissed within the last four years, the loan casefile will receive an Ineligible recommendation and will be ineligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If a non-Chapter 13 bankruptcy was filed, discharged, or dismissed within the last four years, the loan casefile will receive an Ineligible recommendation and will be ineligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: DU will not take bankruptcy information in the public record section of the credit report into account if the bankruptcy is dated more than seven years prior to the credit report date.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: DU will not take tradeline accounts that are reported with a bankruptcy status code or manner of payment (MOP) code of '7' into account if there is at least one bankruptcy reported in a public record within seven years of the credit report date. In this scenario, DU assumes the date filed and the date discharged in the public record are more accurate than the dates in the tradeline; i.e., specific filed and discharged dates do not exist in the tradeline.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: DU will use tradeline accounts that are reported with a bankruptcy status code or MOP code of '7' if there is not a bankruptcy reported in a public record within seven years of the credit report date. In this scenario, the lender will need to verify the actual filed and discharged dates to determine that the bankruptcy meets the DU bankruptcy policy.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: DU is not able to determine if multiple filings have occurred due to the manner in which bankruptcies are reported to the credit report. DU will issue a message when it appears that there may have been multiple bankruptcy filings. This message will list each of the bankruptcies seen on the credit report, and will instruct lenders to ensure the loan casefile meets the criteria for underwriting loan casefiles with multiple bankruptcies.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Underwriting when the Credit Report Contains Inaccurate Bankruptcy Information
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When DU identifies a bankruptcy on the credit report and the information is inaccurate, the lender may instruct DU to disregard the bankruptcy information on the credit report in the eligibility assessment. This is done by entering 'Confirmed CR BK Incorrect' in the online loan application and resubmitting the loan casefile to DU. When the loan casefile is resubmitted to DU, the bankruptcy information on the credit report will not be used.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If the lender enters 'Confirmed CR BK Incorrect', the lender must document that the Chapter 13 bankruptcy was discharged two or more years or dismissed four or more years from the disbursement date of the new loan, or that the non-Chapter 13 bankruptcy was discharged or dismissed four years or more years from the disbursement date of the new loan
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Underwriting when a Bankruptcy Was Due to Extenuating Circumstances
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When DU identifies a bankruptcy on the credit report and that bankruptcy was due to extenuating circumstances, the lender may instruct DU to disregard the bankruptcy information on the credit report in the eligibility assessment. This is done by entering 'Confirmed CR BK EC' in the online loan application and resubmitting the loan casefile to DU. When the loan casefile is resubmitted to DU, the bankruptcy information on the credit report will not be used.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If the lender enters 'Confirmed CR BK EC', the lender must document that the bankruptcy was due to extenuating circumstances, and that the Chapter 13 bankruptcy was dismissed two or more years from the disbursement date of the new loan, or that the non-Chapter 13 bankruptcy was discharged or dismissed two or more years from the disbursement date of the new loan.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Foreclosure
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: DU applies the following guidelines to prior foreclosures:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Mortgage accounts, including first liens, second liens, home improvement loans, HELOCs, and manufactured home loans, will be identified as a foreclosure if there is an MOP code of '8,' or a Remarks Code that indicates a foreclosure is present in the credit report data and associated to the tradeline.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If a foreclosure was reported within the seven-year period prior to the credit report date, the loan casefile will receive an Ineligible recommendation and will be ineligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If the filed date and the satisfied date of the foreclosure are both unknown, but it appears that the foreclosure occurred within the seven-year period prior to the credit report date, the lender must confirm that the foreclosure did not occur within the most recent seven-year period.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Foreclosure laws vary by state and the time it takes to complete the process may vary by state. DU assumes that the date the foreclosure was reported in the tradeline is the date of the foreclosure sale or liquidation. The lender must confirm that all foreclosures are satisfied.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Mortgage accounts that are identified as a deed-in-lieu of foreclosure or preforeclosure sale will not be identified as a foreclosure.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Underwriting when Inaccurate Foreclosure Information Exists
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When DU identifies a foreclosure on a credit report tradeline and the foreclosure information on that tradeline is inaccurate, the lender may instruct DU to disregard the foreclosure information on the credit report in the eligibility assessment. This is done by entering 'Confirmed CR FC Incorrect' in the online loan application and resubmitting the loan casefile to DU. When the loan casefile is resubmitted to DU, the foreclosure information on the credit report tradeline will not be used in the eligibility assessment.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If the lender enters 'Confirmed CR FC Incorrect,' the lender must then document the foreclosure was completed seven or more years from the disbursement date of the new loan, or that the account was not subject to foreclosure and the loan complies with all other applicable requirements.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Underwriting when Extenuating Circumstances Exist
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When DU identifies a foreclosure on a credit report tradeline and that foreclosure was due to extenuating circumstances, the lender may instruct DU to disregard the foreclosure information on the credit report in the eligibility assessment. This is done by entering 'Confirmed CR FC EC' in the online loan application and resubmitting the loan casefile to DU. When the loan casefile is resubmitted to DU, the foreclosure information on the credit report tradeline will not be used in the eligibility assessment.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If the lender enters 'Confirmed CR FC EC,' the lender must then document that the foreclosure was due to extenuating circumstances, the foreclosure was completed three or more years from the disbursement date of the new loan, and the loan complies with all other requirements specific to a foreclosure due to extenuating circumstances.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Deed-in-Lieu of Foreclosure
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: DU applies the following guidelines to prior DILs:
elements.label: text

elements.text: DU will determine if a mortgage tradeline is a DIL by using specific Remarks Codes that are present in the credit report data and associated to the tradeline.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: When DU identifies a DIL, the lender must document that the event was completed four or more years from the disbursement date of the new loan, or two or more years from the disbursement date of the new loan when the lender confirms that the mortgage loan meets the applicable time frames and eligibility requirements for a deed-in-lieu of foreclosure due to extenuating circumstances.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Preforeclosure Sales or Short Sales
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: DU will determine if a mortgage tradeline is a PFS by using specific Remarks Codes that are present in the credit report data and associated to the tradeline.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: When DU identifies a PFS, the lender must document that the event was completed four or more years from the disbursement date of the new loan, or two or more years from the disbursement date of the new loan when the lender confirms that the mortgage loan meets the applicable time frames and eligibility requirements for a preforeclosure sale due to extenuating circumstances.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Charge-Off of Mortgage Accounts
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Mortgage accounts, including first liens, second liens, home improvements loans, HELOCs, and manufactured home loans, will be identified as a charge-off if there is an MOP code of '9' (collection or charge-off) and there is no information indicating the account may also be subject to a foreclosure (MOP code '8' or foreclosure Remarks Code), a deed-in-lieu of foreclosure (DIL Remarks Code), or a preforeclosure sale (PFS Remarks Code).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: When DU identifies a charge-off on a mortgage tradeline, the lender must document that the event was completed four or more years from the disbursement date of the new loan, or two or more years from the disbursement date of the new loan when the lender confirms that the mortgage loan meets the applicable time frames and eligibility requirements for a charge-off due to extenuating circumstances.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: B3-5.4-01, Eligibility Requirements for Loans with Nontraditional Credit (11/05/2025)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on nontraditional credit eligibility requirements, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Overview
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Manual Underwriting
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: DU Loan Casefiles
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Homeownership Education
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Overview
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: If one or more borrowers do not have a credit score or have a limited traditional credit history, the lender must establish an acceptable nontraditional credit history. The lender must first check all three major credit repositories to verify the borrower's credit history.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The credit report will indicate if a credit score could not be produced due to insufficient credit. Lenders must ensure that the credit report accurately reflects the borrower's information, such as the name, Social Security number, and current residence of the borrower to confirm that the lack of traditional credit was not erroneously reported because incorrect information was used to order the credit report.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Manual Underwriting
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: If one or more borrowers on the loan does not have a credit score and is relying on nontraditional credit to qualify, the following requirements apply:
elements.label: text

elements.text: The property must be a one-unit, principal residence.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Non-occupant co-borrowers are permitted, provided the requirements described in B2-2-04, Guarantors, Co-Signers, or Non-Occupant Borrowers on the Subject Transaction, are met in addition to the eligibility requirements described herein.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The transaction must be a purchase or limited cash-out refinance.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The loan amount must meet the baseline loan limits-high-balance mortgage loans are not eligible.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The maximum debt-to-income ratio is 36%.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: There is no minimum reserve requirement if at least one borrower can document a housing payment history as one nontraditional credit reference. Otherwise, a minimum of 12 months reserves is required. See B3-5.4-02, Number and Types of Nontraditional Credit References, for additional information.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: A nontraditional credit history must be documented for each borrower without a credit score. See B3-5.4-03, Documentation and Assessment of a Nontraditional Credit History, for additional information.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Unacceptable Uses
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The establishment of a nontraditional credit history is not acceptable for the following scenarios:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: The lender is able to obtain a credit score for the borrower despite the borrower's limited use of credit.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The borrower has a sufficient amount of credit to obtain a credit score and the representative credit score is less than the minimum required.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Note : An exception is permitted for certain HomeReady loans for borrowers with low credit scores. See B5-6-02, HomeReady Mortgage Underwriting Methods and Requirements, for additional information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The borrower's traditional credit history indicates significant derogatory references, such as a prior bankruptcy or foreclosure. In these cases, the borrower must have re-established credit in accordance with B3-5.3-07, Significant Derogatory Credit Events - Waiting Periods and Re-establishing Credit, including the establishment of traditional credit and a credit score.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: DU Loan Casefiles
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When at least one borrower has a minimum of one credit account or installment account reported on their credit report, DU will assess the risk using the borrower's reported credit and the loan will be subject to standard eligibility guidelines.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When no borrower has at least one credit account or installment account reported on their credit report, DU will apply the following requirements:
elements.label: text

elements.text: The property must be a one- to four-unit, principal residence, and all borrowers must occupy the property.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The transaction must be a purchase or limited cash-out refinance.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Reserves may be required as determined by DU.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: In both cases, no verification of nontraditional credit references is required for any borrower when DU conducts a cash flow assessment and issues a message that the third-party asset verification report may be used to satisfy the requirements. Otherwise, a nontraditional credit history must be documented for each borrower as instructed by DU. See B3-5.4-03, Documentation and Assessment of a Nontraditional Credit History, for additional information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If a loan casefile does not receive an Approve/Eligible recommendation, it may receive a more favorable recommendation if a 12-month asset verification report is obtained (see B3-2-03, Risk Factors Evaluated by DU). In some cases the loan may still be eligible for manual underwriting. The lender must determine whether the loan meets the requirements for a manually underwritten loan that includes a borrower without a credit score.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Homeownership Education
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: See B2-2-06, Homeownership Education and Housing Counseling, for the requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Documentation and Assessment of a Nontraditional Credit History.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Eligible Types of Nontraditional Credit References
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The types of credit references that can be used to develop a nontraditional credit history are those that require the borrower to make periodic payments on a regular basis with intervals that are no longer than every three months.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : There is an exception to allow a longer interval for the payment of real estate taxes on the borrower's principal residence as described below.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender should consider whether the borrower has any eligible housing expenses and then review other nontraditional credit references. The lender must conduct an informational interview with the borrower to identify all credit references covering the most recent consecutive 12 months. If the lender is requesting a nontraditional mortgage credit report from a consumer reporting agency, the agency will conduct the borrower interview and obtain the list of available nontraditional credit references.
elements.label: text

elements.text: In all cases, the payment history for each credit reference must be documented for the most recent consecutive 12-month period. All credit references must be included, not just those that reflect acceptable performance.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Acceptable Housing Payments
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Loans underwritten through DU where a nontraditional credit history is required must include housing payments as one reference of nontraditional credit. Loans underwritten manually are not required to have a housing payment as one reference of nontraditional credit. However, if no borrower on the loan has a housing history, a minimum of 12 months' reserves must be documented.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: The following are examples of acceptable housing payments:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Rent: payments made to a landlord or property management company;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Privately held mortgage loan: housing payments not reported to the credit bureaus, such as contract for deed payments and other similar arrangements, provided the payments are related to the borrower's principal residence; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Real estate taxes: payments made on the principal residence, regardless of payment frequency (for homes owned free and clear).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Other Nontraditional Credit References
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: In addition to a housing payment, the following other nontraditional credit references may be used to further develop additional nontraditional credit history for the borrower:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Utilities, such as electricity, gas, water, telephone service, television, and internet service providers. If utilities are included in the housing payment, they cannot be considered a separate nontraditional credit reference. Utilities can be considered a credit reference only if the payment history can be separately documented.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Medical insurance coverage (excluding payroll deductions)
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Automobile insurance payments
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Cell phone payments
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Life insurance policies (excluding payroll deductions)
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Payments for property or renter's insurance
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Payments to local stores, such as department stores, furniture stores, appliance stores
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Rental (or lease) payments for durable goods, such as automobiles
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Payment of medical bills
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Payment of school tuition
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Payments for childcare
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: A loan obtained from an individual, provided the repayment terms can be documented in a written agreement
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Checking account, savings account, voluntary payments made to a payroll savings plan or contributions to a stock purchase plan, provided the records reflect an increasing balance as a result of periodic deposits over at least the most recent 12 months. Contributions must have been made no less than quarterly.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Wire remittance statements demonstrating a consistent amount of funds remitted over the most recent 12-month period.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: B3-5.4-03, Documentation and Assessment of a Nontraditional Credit History (02/07/2024)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on the documentation and assessment of a nontraditional credit history, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: General Documentation Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Housing Payment History
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Standards for Individual Credit References Obtained Directly from a Creditor
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Standards for Documenting a Nontraditional Payment History Obtained From the Borrower
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Verification of Bank Accounts and Wire Remittance Statements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Borrowers with Disabilities
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Non-U.S. Citizen and Foreign Borrowers
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Assessment of the Payment History for Nontraditional Credit References
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: General Documentation Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender can document the borrower's nontraditional credit history directly from the borrower or the creditor, or by obtaining a nontraditional mortgage credit report from a consumer reporting agency.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Housing Payment History
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The borrower's housing payment history must be documented for the most recent consecutive 12-month period. The following documentation is acceptable:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Canceled checks can be provided. In lieu of canceled checks, the lender may use the borrower's bank statements, copies of money orders, or other reasonable methods for documenting timely housing payments. The documentation must clearly indicate the payee and amount being paid, and reflect that payments were made on a consistent basis.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Direct verification of the payment of rent from the landlord. Direct landlord verification is acceptable whether the landlord is an individual or a professional management company.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: If at least one borrower on the loan can document a housing payment as a nontraditional credit reference, the loan has met the housing payment history requirement. The lender is not required to obtain documentation of a housing payment history for other nontraditional credit borrowers on the loan. However, the lender must still document the minimum number of nontraditional credit references required for each nontraditional credit borrower.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If two or more borrowers on a loan share the housing-related reference (for example, they are both named on the lease for the property in which they are living), that documentation counts as one nontraditional credit reference for each borrower, even if only one borrower has been making the payments.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : If the credit report contains a housing payment reference and it includes the required information, including payment history, then the lender may use that housing payment reference as an acceptable nontraditional credit reference.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For examples of acceptable housing payments to fulfill this housing payment history requirement, see B3-5.4-02, Number and Types of Nontraditional Credit References.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Standards for Individual Credit References Obtained Directly from a Creditor
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Individual credit references (other than housing payments) from a creditor must include the following:
elements.label: text

elements.text: the creditor's name,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the name of the individual providing the reference,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the date the account was opened,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the amount of highest credit,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the current status of the account,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the required payment amount,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the unpaid balance, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the payment history.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The historical status of each account must be stated in a 'number of times past due' format using '0 X 30, 0 X 60, 0 X 90' days late.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : Vague statements such as 'current,' 'satisfactory,' or 'pays as agreed' are not acceptable by themselves.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Standards for Documenting a Nontraditional Payment History Obtained From the Borrower
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: For documentation obtained directly from the borrower, the following standards must be met:
elements.label: text

elements.text: documentation that describes the terms of the debt repayment or contract together with canceled checks or copies of bills marked 'paid' that reflect the borrower's payment history over the most recent consecutive 12 months.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: withdrawals or debits on the borrower's bank statements that show the payee information clearly listed for the creditor and that payments were made on a consistent basis over the most recent consecutive 12 months.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Verification of Bank Accounts and Wire Remittance Statements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Account statements can be used to document a nontraditional credit history, provided they are from the borrower's checking account, savings account, voluntary payments made to a payroll savings plan, or contributions to a stock purchase plan. The account statements must reflect an increasing balance as a result of periodic deposits over at least the most recent consecutive 12-month period, with contributions being made no less than quarterly. If the account statements demonstrate overdraft activity, that information suggests a weakness in the borrower's ability to meet financial obligations. The lender must assess the significance of this information relative to the borrower's overall credit risk.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: If the loan is underwritten by DU and a cash flow assessment is conducted using a third-party asset verification report, different requirements may apply (see B3-2-03, Risk Factors Evaluated by DU).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Wire remittance statements can be used to document a nontraditional credit history, provided they demonstrate a consistent amount of funds being remitted over the most recent consecutive 12-month period.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Borrowers with Disabilities
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: If a borrower with disabilities does not have a credit score and a nontraditional credit history is being developed, the lender may use documentation provided by a court-appointed guardian, a Social Security Administration representative payee, or a parent, provided that this party:
elements.label: text

elements.text: manages the borrower's financial transactions,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: maintains records on the borrower's behalf, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: uses credit accounts held jointly in the name of the person with disabilities to pay financial obligations.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The lender can use the documentation provided either to request a nontraditional mortgage credit report from a consumer reporting agency, or to establish a nontraditional credit history for the borrower, as described in this topic.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Non-U.S. Citizen and Foreign Borrowers
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: If a non-U.S. citizen or foreign borrower lacks sufficient credit references in the United States to satisfy Fannie Mae requirements, the lender must use credit references from foreign countries to achieve the required number of nontraditional credit references and establish a nontraditional credit profile.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Assessment of the Payment History for Nontraditional Credit References
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: For each nontraditional credit reference, the following requirements must be met:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: There cannot be any delinquency on housing payments within the past 12 months.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Only one account, excluding housing payments, can have a 30-day delinquency in the past 12 months.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: No collections (other than medical collections) or judgments have been filed in the past 24 months.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Judgments, liens, collections, and charge-offs of non-mortgage accounts must be satisfied in accordance with  B3-6-07, Debts Paid Off At or Prior to Closing (for manually underwritten loans), or B3-5.3-09, DU Credit Report Analysis (for loans underwritten with DU).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Note : A borrower may lack sufficient credit to obtain a credit score. However, the lender must still consider any derogatory credit references that appear on the credit report.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Chapter B3-6, Liability Assessment
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: text

elements.text: This chapter describes liability assessment for qualifying, underwriting, and documentation purposes.
elements.label: text

elements.text: B3-6-01, General Information on Liabilities (05/04/2022)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on liabilities, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: General Information on Liabilities
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Monthly Obligations Not Included in Liabilities
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: General Information on Liabilities
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: The lender's risk analysis must include all liabilities affecting income or assets that will affect the borrower's ability to fulfill the mortgage payment obligation.
elements.label: text

elements.text: A borrower's liabilities include the following:
elements.label: text

elements.text: housing payment (mortgage or rent) for each borrower's principal residence,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: all revolving charge accounts,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: installment loan debts with a remaining payment term greater than 10 months,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: installment debts secured by virtual currency,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: lease payments,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: real estate loans,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: HELOCs,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: alimony and child support,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: maintenance payments, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: all other debts of a recurring nature.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: For each liability, the lender must determine the unpaid balance, the terms of repayment, and the borrower's payment history, and verify any other liability that is not shown on a credit report by obtaining documentation from the borrower or creditor.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the credit report does not contain a reference for each significant open debt shown on the loan application-including outstanding mortgage debt, bank, student, or credit union loans-the lender must provide separate credit verification.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If a current liability appears on the credit report that is not shown on the loan application, the borrower should provide a reasonable explanation for the undisclosed debt. Documentation may be required to support the borrower's explanation.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the borrower discloses, or the lender discovers, additional liabilities after the underwriting decision has been made, up to and concurrent with closing, the lender must recalculate the borrower's debt-to-income ratio.
elements.label: text

elements.text: See B3-6-02, Debt-to-Income Ratios and B3-6-03, Monthly Housing Expense for the Subject Property for additional information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Monthly Obligations Not Included in Liabilities
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Some obligations, often identified on a borrower's paystub, are not considered a liability and will not be included as a debt or deducted from the borrower's gross income when calculating the borrower's debt-to-income ratio. These obligations include items such as
elements.label: text

elements.text: federal, state, and local taxes;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Federal Insurance Contributions Act (FICA) or other retirement contributions, such as 401(k) accounts (including repayment of debt secured by these funds);
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: commuting costs;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: union dues; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: voluntary deductions.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: B3-6-02, Debt-to-Income Ratios (04/02/2025)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on the use of the debt-to-income (DTI) ratio, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: DTI Ratios
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Maximum DTI Ratios
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Exceptions to the Maximum DTI Ratio
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Calculating Total Monthly Obligation
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: DTI Ratio Tolerance and Re-Underwriting Criteria
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Applying the Re-underwriting Criteria
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: DTI Ratios
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The DTI ratio consists of two components:
elements.label: text

elements.text: total monthly obligations, which includes the qualifying payment for the subject mortgage loan and other long-term and significant short-term monthly debts (see Calculating Total Monthly Obligation below); and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: total monthly income of all borrowers, to the extent the income is used to qualify for the mortgage (see Chapter B3-3, Income Assessment).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Maximum DTI Ratios
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: For manually underwritten loans, Fannie Mae's maximum total DTI ratio is 36% of the borrower's stable monthly income. The maximum can be exceeded up to 45% if the borrower meets the credit score and reserve requirements reflected in the Eligibility Matrix.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: For loan casefiles underwritten through DU, the maximum allowable DTI ratio is 50%.
elements.label: text

elements.text: See B3-1-01, Comprehensive Risk Assessment for information about the DTI.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Exceptions to the Maximum DTI Ratio
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Fannie Mae makes exceptions to the maximum allowable DTI ratios for particular mortgage transactions, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: cash-out refinance transactions - the maximum ratio may be lower for loan casefiles underwritten through DU (see B2-1.3-03, Cash-Out Refinance Transactions);
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: high LTV refinance transactions - except for loans underwritten under the Alternative Qualification Path, there are no maximum DTI ratio requirements (see B5-7-01, High LTV Refinance Loan and Borrower Eligibility);
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: borrowers who do not have a credit score - the maximum ratio may be lower for manually underwritten loans (see B3-5.4-01, Eligibility Requirements for Loans with Nontraditional Credit);
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: non-occupant borrowers - the maximum ratio is lower than 45% for the occupying borrower for manually underwritten loans (see B2-2-04, Guarantors, Co-Signers, or Non-Occupant Borrowers on the Subject Transaction); and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: government mortgage loans - lenders must follow the requirements for the respective government agency.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Calculating Total Monthly Obligation
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The total monthly obligation is the sum of the following:
elements.label: text

elements.text: the housing payment for each borrower's principal residence
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: if the subject loan is the borrower's principal residence, use the PITIA and qualifying payment amount (see B3-6-03, Monthly Housing Expense for the Subject Property);
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: if there is a non-occupant borrower, use the mortgage payment (including HOA fees and subordinate lien payments) or rental payments (see B3-6-05, Monthly Debt Obligations);
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: if the subject loan is a second home or investment property, use the mortgage payment (including HOA fees and subordinate lien payments) or rental payments (see B3-6-05, Monthly Debt Obligations;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the qualifying payment amount if the subject loan is for a second home or investment property (see B3-6-04, Qualifying Payment Requirements);
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: monthly payments on installment debts and other mortgage debts that extend beyond ten months;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: monthly payments on installment debts and other mortgage debts that extend ten months or less if the payments significantly affect the borrower's ability to meet credit obligations;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: monthly payments on installment debts secured by virtual currency;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: monthly payments on revolving debts;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: monthly payments on lease agreements, regardless of the expiration date of the lease;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: monthly alimony, child support, or maintenance payments that extend beyond ten months (alimony (but not child support or maintenance) may instead be deducted from income, (see B3-6-05, Monthly Debt Obligations);
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: monthly payments for other recurring monthly obligations; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: any net loss from a rental property.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Note : Fannie Mae acknowledges that lenders may sometimes apply a more conservative approach when qualifying borrowers. This is acceptable as long as Fannie Mae's minimum requirements are met, and lenders consistently apply the same approach to similar loans. For example, a lender might calculate a higher minimum payment on a credit card account than what Fannie Mae requires, which is acceptable as long as the lender consistently applies this calculation to all mortgage applications with revolving debts.
elements.label: text

elements.text: DTI Ratio Tolerance and Re-Underwriting Criteria
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Fannie Mae expects lenders to have in place processes to facilitate borrower disclosure of changes in financial circumstances throughout the origination process and prefunding quality control processes to increase the likelihood of discovering material undisclosed debts or reduced income. See D1-2-01, Lender Prefunding Quality Control Review Process.
elements.label: text

elements.text: As a result of the lender's normal processes and controls, the lender may need to re-underwrite the loan after initial underwriting. If the borrower discloses or the lender discovers additional debt(s) or reduced income after the underwriting decision was made up to and concurrent with loan closing, the loan must be re-underwritten if the new information causes the DTI ratio to increase by more than the allowed tolerances.
elements.label: text

elements.text: In all cases, if the lender determines that there is new subordinate financing on the subject property during the loan process, the mortgage loan must be re-underwritten.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: Re-underwriting means that loan casefiles must be resubmitted to DU with updated information; and for manually underwritten loans, a comprehensive risk and eligibility assessment must be performed.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Applying the Re-underwriting Criteria
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The following steps are required if the borrower discloses or the lender discovers additional debt(s) or reduced income after the underwriting decision was made up to and concurrent with loan closing:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Step
elements.label: text

elements.text: Description
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender must document the additional debt(s) and reduced income in accordance with
elements.label: text

elements.text: B3-6-01, General Information on Liabilities or B3-3, Income Assessment, as applicable.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note
elements.label: text

elements.text: : The lender is not required to obtain a new credit report to verify the additional debt(s).
elements.label: text

elements.text: However, if the lender chooses to obtain a new credit report after the initial underwriting decision
elements.label: text

elements.text: was made, the loan must be re-underwritten.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If there is new subordinate debt on the subject property, the mortgage loan must be re-
elements.label: text

elements.text: underwritten.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Step
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Description
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: 3 The lender must recalculate the DTI ratio. For DU loan casefiles, the DTI ratio should be recalculated outside of DU.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If the recalculated DTI ratio exceeds 45% for a manually underwritten loan or 50% for a DU loan casefile, the loan is not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Manually underwritten loans: If the recalculated DTI does not exceed 45%, the mortgage loan must be re-underwritten with the updated information to determine if the loan is still eligible for delivery. Note: If the increase in the DTI ratio moves the DTI ratio above the 36% threshold, the loan must meet the credit score and reserve requirements in the Eligibility Matrix that apply to DTI ratios greater than 36% up to 45%.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: DU loan casefiles: See B3-2-10, Accuracy of DU Data, DU Tolerances, and Errors in the Credit Report for the tolerances and resubmission requirements associated with changes impacting the DTI.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: High LTV refinance loans: For loans underwritten in accordance with the Alternative Qualification Path, if the recalculated DTI ratio exceeds 45%, the loan is not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae. If the DTI does not exceed 45%, but is increasing by 3 or more percentage points, the loan must be re-underwritten with the updated information to determine if the loan is still eligible for delivery.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: 5 The final loan application signed by the borrower must include all income and debts verified, disclosed, or identified during the mortgage process.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: 6 Upon delivery to Fannie Mae, the lender must deliver the qualifying monthly income and expense amounts that are on the final loan application. See C1-2-02, Loan Data and Documentation Delivery Requirements.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: B3-6-03, Monthly Housing Expense for the Subject Property (12/16/2020)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information about the following:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Monthly Housing Expense
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Calculating Monthly Real Estate Tax Payment
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Monthly Housing Expense
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Monthly housing expense is the sum of the following and is referred to as PITIA for the subject property:
elements.label: text

elements.text: principal and interest (P&I);
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: property, flood, and mortgage insurance premiums (as applicable);
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: real estate taxes;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: ground rent;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: special assessments;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: any owners' association dues (including utility charges that are attributable to the common areas, but excluding any utility charges that apply to the individual unit);
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: any monthly co-op corporation fee (less the pro rata share of the master utility charges for servicing individual units that is attributable to the borrower's unit);
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: any subordinate financing payments on mortgages secured by the subject property.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Note: The monthly payment of a subordinate lien associated with a business debt secured by the subject property can be excluded from the monthly housing expense if it meets the requirements of Business Debt in the Borrower's Name in B3-6-05, Monthly Debt Obligations.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lenders must enter all components of the monthly housing expense on the loan application including subordinate financing P&I, homeowner's insurance, supplemental property insurance, real estate taxes, mortgage insurance, association/project dues, and other proposed housing expenses.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: If the subject mortgage is secured by the borrower's principal residence, the monthly housing expense is based on the qualifying payment required in accordance with B3-6-04, Qualifying Payment Requirements. This amount is the monthly housing expense used to calculate the debt-to-income (DTI) ratio.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the subject mortgage is secured by a second home or an investment property, the qualifying payment amount is considered one of the borrower's monthly debt obligations when calculating the DTI ratio.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Calculating Monthly Real Estate Tax Payment
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender must base its calculation of real estate taxes for borrower qualification on no less than the current assessed value. However, the lender must project the real estate taxes if one of the following applies:
elements.label: text

elements.text: For purchase and construction-related transactions, the lender must use a reasonable estimate of the real estate taxes based on the value of the land and the total of all new and existing improvements. This policy also applies to properties in jurisdictions where a transfer of ownership typically results in a reassessment or revaluation of the property and a corresponding increase in the amount of taxes.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: There is a tax abatement on the subject property that will last for no less than 5 years from the note date. For example:
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: for a municipality with a 10-year abatement, the lender may qualify the borrower with the reduced tax amount;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: for a municipality with a 10-year abatement and with annual real estate tax increases in years 1 through 10, the lender must qualify the borrower with the annual taxes that will be required at the end of the 5 th year after the first mortgage payment date.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The lender has the option to project the real estate taxes if the amount of taxes will be reduced based on federal, state, or local jurisdictional requirements. However, the taxes may not be reduced if an appeal to reduce them is only pending and has not been approved.
elements.label: text

elements.text: B3-6-05, Monthly Debt Obligations (06/03/2026)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic describes obligations that should be considered in underwriting the loan, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Alimony, Child Support, Equalization Payments, and Separate Maintenance Payments
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Bridge / Swing Loans
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Business Debt in Borrower's Name
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Court-Ordered Assignment of Debt
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Debts Paid by Others
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Non-Applicant Accounts
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Deferred Installment Debt
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Federal Income Tax Installment Agreements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Garnishments
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Home Equity Lines of Credit
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Installment Debt
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Lease Payments
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Rental Housing Payment
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Loans Secured by Financial Assets
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Open 30-Day Charge Accounts
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Other Real Estate Owned-Qualifying Impact
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Revolving Charge/Lines of Credit
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Student Loans
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Alimony, Child Support, Equalization Payments, and Separate Maintenance Payments When the borrower is required to pay alimony, child support, equalization payments, or separate maintenance payments under a divorce decree, separation agreement, or any other written legal agreement-and those payments must continue to be made for more than ten months-the payments must be considered as part of the borrower's recurring monthly debt obligations. However, voluntary payments do not need to be taken into consideration and an exception is allowed for alimony. A copy of the divorce decree, separation agreement, court order, or equivalent documentation confirming the amount of the obligation must be obtained and retained in the loan file.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: For alimony, equalization payments, and separate maintenance obligations, the lender has the option to reduce the qualifying income by the amount of the obligation in lieu of including it as a monthly payment in the calculation of the DTI ratio.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : For loan casefiles underwritten through DU, when using the option of reducing the borrower's monthly qualifying income by the alimony, equalization payments, or separate maintenance payment, the lender must enter the amount of the monthly obligation as a negative alimony or separate maintenance income amount. (If the borrower also receives alimony, equalization payments, or separate maintenance income, the amounts should be combined and entered as a net amount.)
elements.label: text

elements.text: Bridge / Swing Loans
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When a borrower obtains a bridge (or swing) loan, the funds from that loan can be used for closing on a new principal residence before the current residence is sold. This creates a contingent liability that must be considered part of the borrower's recurring monthly debt obligations and included in the DTI ratio calculation.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae will waive this requirement and not require the debt to be included in the DTI ratio if the following documentation is provided:
elements.label: text

elements.text: a fully executed sales contract for the current residence, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: confirmation that any financing contingencies have been cleared.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Business Debt in Borrower's Name
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When a self-employed borrower claims that a monthly obligation that appears on their personal credit report (such as a Small Business Administration loan) is being paid by the borrower's business, the lender must confirm that it verified that the obligation was actually paid out of company funds and that this was considered in its cash flow analysis of the borrower's business.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The account payment does not need to be considered as part of the borrower's DTI ratio if:
elements.label: text

elements.text: the account in question does not have a history of delinquency,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the business provides acceptable evidence that the obligation was paid out of company funds (such as 12 months of canceled company checks), and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the lender's cash flow analysis of the business took payment of the obligation into consideration.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The account payment must be considered as part of the borrower's DTI ratio in any of the following situations:
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the business does not provide sufficient evidence that the obligation was paid out of company funds.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If the business provides acceptable evidence of its payment of the obligation, but the lender's cash flow analysis of the business does not reflect any business expense related to the obligation (such as an
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: interest expense-and taxes and insurance, if applicable-equal to or greater than the amount of interest that one would reasonably expect to see given the amount of financing shown on the credit report and the age of the loan). It is reasonable to assume that the obligation has not been accounted for in the cash flow analysis.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the account in question has a history of delinquency. To ensure that the obligation is counted only once, the lender should adjust the net income of the business by the amount of interest, taxes, or insurance expense, if any, that relates to the account in question.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Court-Ordered Assignment of Debt
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When a borrower has outstanding debt that was assigned to another party by court order (such as under a divorce decree or separation agreement) and the creditor does not release the borrower from liability, the borrower has a contingent liability. The lender is not required to count this contingent liability as part of the borrower's recurring monthly debt obligations.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender is not required to evaluate the payment history for the assigned debt after the effective date of the assignment. The lender cannot disregard the borrower's payment history for the debt before its assignment.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Debts Paid by Others
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Certain debts can be excluded from the borrower's recurring monthly obligations and the DTI ratio:
elements.label: text

elements.text: When a borrower is obligated on a non-mortgage debt - but is not the party who is actually repaying the debt - the lender may exclude the monthly payment from the borrower's recurring monthly obligations. This policy applies whether or not the other party is obligated on the debt, but is not applicable if the other party is an interested party to the subject transaction (such as the seller or real estate agent). Nonmortgage debts include installment loans, student loans, revolving accounts, lease payments, alimony, child support, and separate maintenance. See below for treatment of payments due under a federal income tax installment agreement.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: When a borrower is obligated on a mortgage debt - but is not the party who is actually repaying the debt
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the lender may exclude the full monthly housing expense (PITIA) from the borrower's recurring monthly obligations if
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the party making the payments is obligated on the mortgage debt,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: there are no delinquencies in the most recent 12 months, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the borrower is not using rental income from the applicable property to qualify.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: In order to exclude non-mortgage debts, including when the borrower is an authorized user on a revolving account, or mortgage debts from the borrower's DTI ratio, the lender must obtain the most recent 12 months' canceled checks (or bank statements) from the other party making the payments that document a 12-month payment history with no delinquent payments.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When a borrower is obligated on a mortgage debt, regardless of whether or not the other party is making the monthly mortgage payments, the referenced property must be included in the count of financed properties (if applicable per B2-2-03, Multiple Financed Properties for the Same Borrower.
